meta.scope: A complete, field-ready setup for a Bay Area landscape/nature shooter and content creator — configure once, then switch between eight scenarios via the mode dial (MR 1/2/3), the M1–M4 card registers, and assigned buttons.
meta.updated: Every menu name, path, and option verified against Sony’s official ILCE-7M5 Help Guide. Recommended values are tuned for you.

stages.title: The five-layer mental model
stages.subtitle: Read this once. It’s why the order matters.
stages.blurb: Think of the camera as five layers, configured bottom-up. Each layer depends on the ones below it — so we build from the foundation and finish at recallable presets.

blocks.heading: What this body actually is (per Sony’s manual)
body: A 33.0-megapixel full-frame (35.9 × 23.9 mm) body. These are the headline capabilities the whole guide leans on — all confirmed in the Help Guide:
• Continuous shooting: up to 10 fps with the mechanical shutter, up to 30 fps with the electronic shutter (Hi+). You can also pick 20 / 15 / 10 / 5 fps.
• Pre-Capture: buffers 0.03 – 1.0 second before you fully press — electronic shutter + continuous drive only.
• ISO 100–51200 (extended 50–204800); AF works down to −4 EV (F2, Single-shot AF).
• Subject recognition targets: Human · Animal/Bird · Animal · Bird · Insect · Car/Train · Airplane (plus Auto).
• Video: 4K up to 120p (S35 crop); full-frame 4K 60p needs [4K angle of view Priority]; FHD up to 240 fps in S&Q. There’s also an in-camera Time-lapse movie mode.
• Two slots: Slot 1 takes CFexpress Type A or SD; Slot 2 is SD only.

blocks.heading: Your two-card strategy
body: Put a CFexpress Type A (VPG200) in Slot 1 and your SanDisk V90 in Slot 2.

CFexpress Type A is the only card that covers every mode — S&Q XAVC S-I 4K (1200 Mbps) and S&Q XAVC S-I HD (890 Mbps) are CFexpress-only per the card table. Everything up to XAVC S-I 4K (600 Mbps) also runs on a V90 SD.

Use Slot 1 (CFx-A) as primary and Slot 2 (V90 SD) as a [Simult. Recording] backup — but know that recording to both throttles to the slower SD card, and at Hi+ the Lossless-Comp buffer is only ~35 frames, so burst in short taps.

blocks.heading: The five layers
body: Layer 1 — One-time housekeeping. Region, date/time, file naming, copyright, card format, colour space, power/thermal. Set once, essentially never touch.
Layer 2 — Global stills + global video defaults. Your “pick it up cold” settings on the P/A/S/M dial positions. When an MR preset doesn’t cover a situation, you fall back here.

Layer 3 — The autofocus system. Focus mode, area, subject recognition, and the AF-C tuning sliders. Recognition target and the AF-C tuning live in each scenario preset. AF is stored separately for Still and Movie.
Layer 4 — Custom controls (buttons / dials / Fn / My Menu). The Custom Key/Dial map is separate for Still, Movie, and Playback. This is how a pro makes a deep menu disappear.

Layer 5 — Recallable scenario presets (MR 1/2/3 + M1–M4). [Camera Set. Memory] writes the entire camera state into a slot — three in-body (1/2/3) and four on the card (M1–M4). Rotate the dial and the camera reconfigures in well under a second.

blocks.heading: What the manual does NOT tell you
body: To keep this guide honest: Sony’s Help Guide does not publish a dynamic-range figure, an IBIS “stops” rating, a base/dual-ISO number, an AF-point count, or a “stacked sensor / oversampling” claim.

You’ll see those numbers in reviews (PetaPixel, CineD, Photons to Photos), and they may well be true — but they’re outside the manual, so this guide doesn’t treat them as fact. Where a recommendation depends on one, it’s flagged.

blocks.heading: A caveat Sony’s UI imposes
body: When the dial sits on MR, the displayed “mode” is whatever you saved (M / A / etc.). You can’t change the base exposure mode of a recalled preset without re-saving it.
The camera won’t show you a label for each slot — so keep a written cheat-sheet. (This app generates one for you at the end.)

stages.title: Housekeeping & Cards
stages.subtitle: Layer 1 · do this first
stages.blurb: This layer makes the camera safe, predictable, and fast to offload. Do it first — some later choices (file system, dual-slot behaviour) depend on it.

groups.title: Region, time & recording media
groups.subtitle: Get these right before you format anything.

gate.headline: We’re heading into the menus
gate.detail: These live in the Setup and Shooting tabs and don’t depend on the Mode dial. Just sit in stills so the menu layout matches.
gate.note: Power on. Battery in, both cards seated.

items.name: NTSC/PAL Selector
items.why: NTSC/PAL Selector sets the camera's entire video frame-rate family: NTSC gives you 24/30/60/120/240 fps, PAL gives 25/50/100/200 fps. It's a global switch that touches every movie and S&Q mode, and the camera forces a card reformat whenever you flip it, so it's a 'set once and forget' decision.
items.example: You're shooting a 4K wildlife clip for a US-distributed YouTube video and want clean 24p and 60p slow-motion; NTSC gives you those rates, whereas PAL would lock you to 25/50 and could introduce judder for North American playback.
items.alt: PAL — choose it if your deliverables are for European broadcast/clients or you shoot under 50 Hz mains lighting and want 25/50 fps to avoid flicker banding.

items.name: Area/Date/Time Setting
items.why: Area/Date/Time Setting stamps every file with the correct local time and time zone, and the [Daylight Savings] On/Off toggle inside it shifts the clock by an hour so timestamps stay honest across the spring/fall changes. Accurate EXIF time is what lets editing software order frames and merge files from multiple cards correctly.
items.example: On a multi-day Sierra trip you shoot a sunrise timelapse on two bodies and offload everything to Lightroom; matching timestamps keep the frames in true chronological order and let you sync clips by capture time instead of guessing.

items.name: Format
items.why: Format erases a card and rebuilds the camera's image database fresh, which prevents corruption and write slowdowns that plague cards formatted on a computer. The A7 V formats one slot at a time (there's no 'both cards' option), so you run it once for Slot 1 and again for Slot 2, and doing so also wipes the M1-M4 recall registers stored on that card.
items.correction: The manual: [Format] targets one slot per run (press the Delete button for a full format). There is no single “format both” option.
items.example: Before a dawn raptor shoot you wipe both your CFexpress and SD cards in-camera so the buffer never chokes mid burst; afterward you're not fighting a 'recording unavailable' error because the database stayed clean.

items.name: Recording Media (still)
items.why: Recording Media for stills set to [Simult. Recording] writes every frame to both card slots at once, giving you an instant in-camera backup of every shot. If one card fails or gets damaged in the field, you still have a complete identical copy on the other.
items.alt: Sort (RAW/JPEG) or Slot 1 — pick this when you'd rather route RAW to the fast card and JPEG to the other, or maximize total capacity over redundancy on a low-stakes shoot.
items.example: You drive four hours to photograph a rare migrant bird and nail the shot once; with simultaneous recording a corrupt SD card on the way home doesn't cost you the image, because the CFexpress holds a duplicate.

items.name: Recording Media (movie)
items.why: Recording Media for movie picks which single slot your video writes to, and high-bitrate 4K and S&Q footage needs the fast CFexpress card to sustain those data rates without dropping frames. Movie offers Slot 1 / Slot 2 / Simult. Recording only (no RAW/JPEG-style sort).
items.example: Recording a 4K 120p S&Q wildlife sequence at several hundred Mbps, a slower SD card would stutter or stop, but routing to the CFexpress in Slot 1 keeps the clip rolling cleanly.
items.alt: Simult. Recording — choose it for a paid hybrid job where losing the take is unacceptable and you want a redundant movie backup, accepting that you're then limited to what both cards can sustain.

items.name: Auto Switch Media Card
items.why: Auto Switch Media Card lets recording spill over to the other slot automatically when the active card fills up or is missing, so capture never just stops. Factory default is Off; turning it On adds a safety net for long unattended sessions.
items.example: During an all-night Milky Way nightlapse the target card hits capacity at 3 a.m.; with this On the camera quietly continues on the second card instead of ending the sequence with a gap.
items.alt: Off (default) — leave it off when you deliberately want shooting to halt at a card boundary, e.g. to keep one event strictly on one card for offload.

groups.title: Files, colour, power & linking
groups.subtitle: The set-and-forget identity of your files.

gate.headline: Still in the menus
gate.detail: Same dial state — we’re moving to the File, Image Quality/Rec, Setup and Power tabs.

items.name: File/Folder Settings
items.value: Set File Name → 3-char prefix; Folder Name → Date Form
items.why: File/Folder Settings controls how your stills are named and grouped: [Set File Name] defines the first three characters of every filename, and [Folder Name] -> Date Form makes each day's shots land in a date-stamped folder. Together they keep imports tidy and prevent filename collisions across cards and bodies. Movies are named separately under [File Settings].
items.example: You set the prefix to 'TAM' for your Tamron-equipped body so its files never clash with your other camera's, and Date Form folders mean a week of Yosemite shooting drops into clean per-day folders in Lightroom.

items.name: Copyright Info
items.why: Copyright Info with [Write Copyright Info] On embeds your name and copyright string into each file's EXIF metadata, traveling with the image wherever it's shared. You fill in [Set Photographer] and [Set Copyright] once and it's baked into every capture.
items.example: A landscape frame you post gets scraped and reused; the embedded copyright EXIF is concrete proof of authorship and makes your case for takedowns or licensing far stronger.

items.name: Color Space
items.why: Color Space tags JPEG/HEIF files with either sRGB or the wider-gamut AdobeRGB, which affects how saturated greens and reds are described; it has no effect on RAW pixel data (only the tag). AdobeRGB-tagged files are prefixed with an underscore. Factory default is sRGB.
items.example: Preparing a print of a vivid autumn aspen scene, AdobeRGB preserves the deep yellows and greens that sRGB would clip, giving the lab a richer gamut to work from; you then convert to sRGB/Rec.709 for web export.
items.alt: sRGB (default) — keep it if your output is web/social only and you'd rather avoid the color-management and conversion steps, since sRGB is the safe universal display space.

items.name: Auto Power OFF Temp.
items.why: Auto Power OFF Temp. sets how hot the camera is allowed to get before it shuts down to protect itself; [High] raises that threshold so long sessions run further before the thermal cutoff. Factory default is Standard, and Sony advises a tripod at the High setting because the body becomes warm to hold.
items.example: During a two-hour summer nightlapse or extended 4K recording, Standard might trip the overheat shutdown mid-sequence, whereas High lets the camera run to the end of the run on its tripod.
items.alt: Standard (default) — use it for handheld shooting where you don't want the body getting uncomfortably hot, since the camera errs toward cooler operation.

items.name: Auto Pixel Mapping
items.why: Auto Pixel Mapping recalibrates the sensor at power-off, remapping hot or dead pixels so they don't show as bright specks in your images. It's especially valuable for long exposures where sensor defects become visible against dark skies. Factory default is On, and Sony says to normally leave it On.
items.example: After a night of 20-second astro exposures, auto pixel mapping cleans up stray hot pixels that would otherwise dot the black sky like fake stars in your Milky Way frames.

items.name: USB Power Supply
items.why: USB Power Supply lets the camera draw power from a connected USB-C source to run or charge during shooting. There is no plain 'On': [Auto] (the factory default) uses whichever connected USB-C port is supplying power, or you can pin it to PORT1/PORT2.
items.correction: No “On” option exists — [Auto] is what supplies power. PORT 1 is 10 Gbps; both ports support USB-PD.
items.example: Running an unattended dawn-to-dusk timelapse, you tether the body to a USB-PD power bank and [Auto] keeps it fed all day so the internal battery never dies mid-sequence.
items.alt: PORT1 or PORT2 — specify a port when you have accessories on both USB-C connectors and want to control exactly which one feeds the camera.

items.name: Different Set for Still/Mv
items.value: Check: ISO, White Balance, Picture Profile, Focus Mode (and others you want split)
items.why: Different Set for Still/Mv is a checklist (not an On/Off toggle) where each checked item is remembered separately for stills versus movie mode, so switching modes doesn't clobber your settings. Checkable items include Aperture, Shutter Speed, ISO, Exposure Comp., Metering Mode, White Balance, Picture Profile, and Focus Mode.
items.correction: The manual: this is a checkbox list of 8 items, not a single On/Off toggle.
items.example: You shoot stills at ISO 100 with a standard profile, then flip to movie where you want ISO 800 and an S-Log3 Picture Profile; with those items checked, jumping between stills and video keeps each mode's values intact instead of forcing you to reset every time.

stages.title: Global Stills Defaults
stages.subtitle: Layer 2 · your “pick it up cold” stills
stages.blurb: These are your defaults on the P/A/S/M dial positions. Everything an MR preset doesn’t override falls back here.

gate.headline: Set the dials for stills defaults
gate.detail: We’re editing the P/A/S/M baseline — sit on Manual so every exposure control is visible.

items.name: File Format
items.why: Chooses whether the camera saves an unprocessed RAW, a finished JPEG/HEIF, or both. RAW keeps all the sensor data so you can rescue highlights/shadows and reset white balance later — the latitude this whole guide assumes. Options: RAW / RAW & JPEG / RAW & HEIF / JPEG / HEIF.
items.example: A high-contrast sunrise over the Marin Headlands — RAW recovers a blown sky and lifts foreground shadows a JPEG would have clipped for good.
items.alt: RAW & JPEG — when you want a ready-to-post JPEG straight off the card plus the RAW for serious edits. Costs card space and trims the burst buffer.

items.name: RAW File Type
items.why: Sets how the RAW is compressed. Lossless Comp discards nothing yet shrinks the file — the quality choice for tripod work. The three options are Lossless Comp / Compressed(HQ) / Compressed; the lossy two write faster and shoot deeper bursts.
items.example: A 16-bit star stack at high ISO — Lossless avoids the faint tone-step artifacts lossy compression can leave in a smooth night sky.
items.alt: Compressed — for 30 fps wildlife, the smaller files roughly triple buffer depth (~95 vs ~35 frames at e-shutter); the quality loss is invisible in real shots. Each scenario preset picks the right type.
items.correction: This body has NO “Uncompressed” and NO “Lightweight” RAW — only Lossless Comp / Compressed(HQ) / Compressed.

items.name: Drive Mode
items.why: Sets what one shutter press does — a single frame, a burst, a self-timer delay, or a bracket. Single is the neutral default; scenario presets set their own. The burst SPEED (Hi+/Hi/Mid/Lo, or 30/20/15/10/5 fps on the electronic shutter) is a separate item, [Cont. Shooting Speed].
items.example: A 20-second tripod exposure of the Bay Bridge wants Single + a 2-sec self-timer so your finger press doesn’t shake the frame; a diving osprey wants Cont. Shooting: Hi+.
items.correction: There’s no “Continuous Hi” drive item — pick [Cont. Shooting], then set speed under [Cont. Shooting Speed].

items.name: Shutter Type
items.why: Selects whether each exposure ends with a physical curtain or a fully electronic readout. Mechanical is the right baseline: it carries the body’s full dynamic range and avoids the rolling-shutter skew and LED banding the electronic shutter can show. Options: Mechanical / Electronic.
items.example: A windy long exposure of crashing surf stays clean on Mechanical; a silent 30 fps burst of a hummingbird needs Electronic (set per preset).
items.alt: Electronic Shutter — silent operation for skittish wildlife, 30 fps bursts, and speeds to 1/16000 wide-open in bright light. Note it disables flash, Long Exposure NR, e-Front Curtain and Anti-flicker.

items.name: Aspect Ratio
items.why: The shape of the recorded image. 3:2 uses the entire sensor; 4:3/16:9/1:1 crop pixels away in-camera. Crucially, RAW is ALWAYS recorded 3:2 no matter what — this setting only crops the JPEG/HEIF and the live-view guide. The factory default is 3:2.
items.example: A coastline you think you want as 16:9 — shoot 3:2 at full resolution and crop to 16:9 later, keeping the freedom to reframe.
items.alt: 1:1 if you compose specifically for square social posts and want the in-finder crop guide while shooting.

items.name: Silent Mode Settings
items.value: Off (On for skittish wildlife / quiet venues)
items.why: A master "go quiet" switch that forces the electronic shutter and silences operation sounds — more thorough than just setting Shutter Type to Electronic. The factory default is Off. Be aware it locks out the mechanical shutter (so you lose the full-DR advantage) and by default suppresses Auto Pixel Mapping.
items.example: Photographing a deer at dawn in a still meadow — Silent Mode On means no mechanical sound at all to spook it.
items.alt: Leave Off for everyday shooting; with Silent Mode on, run Pixel Mapping manually since auto mapping is suppressed.

items.name: Metering Mode
items.why: Chooses which part of the frame the camera reads to set exposure: the whole scene (Multi), centre-weighted, a small Spot, a screen average, or Highlight (biases to protect the brightest areas). Multi is the reliable general default. Options: Multi / Center / Spot (Standard/Large) / Entire Screen Avg. / Highlight.
items.example: A backlit egret against bright water — Spot on the bird exposes it right; Multi would average for the whole scene and underexpose it.
items.alt: Highlight — for high-DR landscapes at sunset, it auto-protects the sky from clipping so you lift the shadows in RAW. Set per scenario.

items.name: ISO AUTO Min. SS
items.why: In P/A with Auto-ISO, this is the slowest shutter the camera will allow before it raises ISO instead — it keeps the shutter fast enough to freeze your subject. Options: Slower / Slow / Standard / Fast / Faster, or an explicit speed. Works only in P and A modes.
items.example: A perched bird that might bolt — set this to 1/1000 and the camera holds at least that speed, bumping ISO rather than risking a blurred 1/125 frame.
items.alt: An explicit speed (e.g. 1/2000) — exact, predictable motion-freezing instead of the relative Fast/Faster steps. Wildlife presets do this.

items.name: High ISO NR
items.why: Reduces high-ISO noise in the JPEG and the embedded preview; its effect on the actual RAW data is minimal. Low keeps fine detail while you do real noise reduction in post. Options: Normal / Low / Off.
items.example: A RAW wildlife frame at ISO 12800 — you’ll clean it with modern AI denoise in Lightroom, so Low avoids the in-camera JPEG smearing fine feather detail.
items.alt: Normal — the default; pick it if you deliver JPEG/HEIF straight from the card and want cleaner files without editing.

items.name: Long Exposure NR
items.why: For exposures ≥1 second, the camera shoots a second equal-length “dark frame” with the shutter closed and subtracts its noise — which doubles the time per shot and locks you out while it processes. Off keeps you shooting; it’s also unavailable with the electronic shutter and in continuous/bracket drive. The factory default is On.
items.example: A sequence of 30× 20-second Milky Way frames — On would make each take 40 s and stall the interval, so turn it Off and use dark frames / stacking in post.
items.alt: On — for a one-off very long single exposure (a 4-minute Bay Bridge frame) where you have time to spare and want the cleanest possible single file with no hot pixels.

items.name: Spot Metering Point
items.why: When you use Spot metering, this decides whether the metering circle stays at the centre of the frame or follows your active focus point. Linking it to the focus point lets you meter the subject itself wherever it sits in the frame.
items.example: An off-centre perched owl in dappled light — Focus Point Link meters the owl wherever you place it, instead of metering empty centre foliage.
items.alt: Center for a predictable, classic meter-then-recompose workflow. (It’s locked to Center in Wide/Zone focus areas.)

items.name: Face Priority in Multi Metering
items.why: When Metering is Multi, this biases exposure toward a detected human face so portraits and pieces-to-camera are well exposed. It only affects Multi metering and only acts when the recognition target is Human. The factory default is On.
items.example: A vlog-style piece-to-camera against a bright window — On keeps your face correctly exposed instead of the camera averaging for the bright background.
items.alt: Off for pure landscape/wildlife so a stray detected face (a hiker in the vista) doesn’t pull your exposure.

items.name: AEL w/ Shutter
items.why: Whether a half-press of the shutter locks exposure. [Auto] locks it for single shots but lets it float during continuous AF bursts; [Off] never locks, so exposure keeps re-metering every frame. The factory default is Auto.
items.example: A bird flying from shade into sun mid-burst — with this Off, each frame re-meters so the bird stays correctly exposed all the way through.
items.alt: On for static recompose-and-shoot landscapes where you want exposure pinned at the point you metered.

items.name: ISO Range Limit
items.why: Caps the range you can dial MANUAL ISO into, so a quick thumb-roll never lands you in the extended values (50, or 102400–204800) where dynamic range and noise suffer. It’s separate from the ISO AUTO range. Leave it open by default, or set a ceiling for peace of mind.
items.example: Set Maximum to ISO 25600 so a fast dial spin during action never drops you onto a mushy ISO 204800 frame by accident.
items.alt: Leave both ends unset (default) if you occasionally want extended ISO 50 for a long daytime exposure.

items.name: Creative Look
items.why: Sony’s in-camera “looks” (ST, PT, NT, VV, VV2, FL, IN, BW…) that tint the JPEG and the live preview. RAW is untouched, so ST keeps your reference neutral while you grade from the RAW. Each look has fine Contrast/Saturation/Sharpness params.
items.example: Want punchy skies and greens straight out of camera for a quick social post? VV2 (Vivid) pops without editing — but your RAW still grades from neutral.
items.alt: VV2 (Vivid) for vivid landscapes, or NT (Neutral) as the flattest JPEG baseline if you finish JPEGs in post.

items.name: SteadyShot
items.why: In-body image stabilisation, On/Off only. On buys you several handheld stops; turn it OFF on a tripod, where a stabiliser hunting against a locked-down body can actually add blur to multi-second exposures.
items.example: Hand-holding at dusk — On lets you keep 1/15 s sharp; mounted for a 20-second astro frame — Off, so IBIS drift doesn’t soften the stars.
items.alt: Off whenever the camera is on a tripod or gimbal for long exposures. Lens-based panning modes live on the lens OSS switch, not here.
items.correction: The body has only On/Off for stills — there’s no “Mode 1/Mode 2” panning setting here; panning modes live on the lens OSS switch.

items.name: Priority Set in AWB
items.why: When White Balance is Auto, this chooses whether the camera keeps the natural warmth of the light (Ambience), forces whites neutral (White), or splits the difference (Standard). The factory default is Standard. For mood-driven landscape light, Ambience preserves the feeling of the scene.
items.example: Golden hour over the headlands or a warm tungsten interior — Ambience keeps the glow instead of Auto neutralising it to a clinical white.
items.alt: White when you need genuinely accurate whites under low-colour-temp light (documentation, product). RAW shooters can fix all of this in post.

items.name: Shutter AWB Lock
items.why: Stops Auto WB from drifting colour frame-to-frame. [Cont. Shooting] locks the white balance to the first frame of a burst so every shot matches; [Shutter Half Press] locks on a half-press. The factory default is Off.
items.example: A wildlife burst as a bird crosses from open sky into shaded trees — Cont. Shooting keeps all frames colour-matched so they batch-edit as one.
items.alt: Shutter Half Press for single-shot colour consistency; also assign AWB Lock to a key for locking WB during video.

stages.title: Global Video Defaults
stages.subtitle: Layer 2 · run-and-gun + YouTube baseline
stages.blurb: Your baseline for run-and-gun and YouTube. Scenario/delivery sections refine these.

gate.headline: Flip to Movie
gate.detail: Many shooting settings — and the Custom Key map — are separate for Movie. Move the lower dial to Movie now.

items.name: File Format (movie)
items.value: XAVC S 4K (edit ease) · XAVC S-I 4K (max quality)
items.why: Picks the movie codec family. XAVC S is Long-GOP H.264 — light to edit, universal, fine for YouTube/Reels. XAVC HS is HEVC (best quality per MB, but heavy to play). XAVC S-I is All-Intra (every frame standalone, best for grading, huge files). Options: XAVC HS 4K / XAVC S 4K / XAVC S HD / XAVC S-I 4K / XAVC S-I HD. The factory default is XAVC S HD.
items.example: A daytime coastal b-roll piece graded in Resolve — XAVC S 4K 4:2:2 10-bit on a fast V60 SD is the sweet spot; reserve XAVC S-I for grade-critical work.
items.alt: XAVC S-I 4K when you grade heavily and want frame-accurate edits — but its bitrates (up to 600 Mbps) need a CFexpress Type A card; XAVC HS 4K for max quality if your machine handles HEVC.

items.name: Movie Settings
items.why: Two sub-settings: [Rec Frame Rate] sets temporal feel (24p is filmic, 30p smoother, 60p slowable) and [Record Setting] sets the bitrate, chroma and bit depth. Pick a 4:2:2 10-bit option for anything you'll grade — it holds far more colour latitude and resists banding in skies.
items.example: A graded sunset clip at 4K 24p 4:2:2 10-bit keeps a clean gradient in the sky; the default 4:2:0 8-bit option would show stepping once you push the grade.
items.alt: 60p when you want to slow full-frame motion ~40% in post without the S35 crop that 4K120 forces. Note: 4K120 is APS-C-cropped and 24p exists only in NTSC.
items.correction: XAVC S-I 4K is All-Intra ~240M (24p)/300M (30p)/600M (60p) — there’s no 600 Mbps at 24p/30p.

items.name: 4K angle of view Priority
items.why: Only affects 4K 60p/50p. On gives true full-sensor width by disabling part of the in-camera noise reduction; Off keeps full NR but narrows the angle of view. For graded landscape b-roll you denoise yourself, On preserves the widest field. The factory default is Off.
items.example: A sweeping full-frame 4K60 vista at base ISO — On gives the widest, sharpest 7K-derived image; for a noisy 4K60 nightscape you’d flip it Off to keep the extra NR.
items.alt: Off when shooting 4K60 in low light (nightscapes), where the extra noise reduction matters more than the slightly wider frame.

items.name: APS-C S35 (Super 35mm) Shooting
items.value: Off (full frame) — toggle On for reach
items.why: Crops the sensor to Super 35 (~1.5×). Off keeps the full-frame field of view and the 7K oversampling you want for landscape; Auto follows the lens. Separate from 4K AoV Priority. Off is the factory default.
items.example: A 24mm stays a true 24mm in full frame (Off); flip to On and it frames like ~36mm for a tighter, compressed coastal shot — or for ~1.5× reach with a tele.
items.alt: On for deliberate extra reach. Note: 4K 120p and 240/200 fps S&Q force the S35 crop regardless — frame those wide. Assign this to a custom key for instant switching.

groups.title: Look & LUT

items.name: Log Shooting Setting
items.why: This is the switch that turns on S-Log3 on the A7 V — set it here, not in Picture Profile. With [Log Shooting] → On (Flexible ISO) the camera records flat S-Log3 with its wide dynamic range, and you set exposure by riding ISO. The factory default is Off, so this single toggle is what puts you into S-Log3.
items.example: A high-contrast sunrise: with Log Shooting On the S-Log3 file holds both the bright clouds and the shadowed foreground, and you grade it later with the s709 (or your own) LUT.
items.alt: Off — only when you want a finished look straight out of camera with no grading; then use PP11 (S-Cinetone) below instead.
items.note: Two sub-options live in the same screen: set Color Gamut → S-Gamut3.Cine, and keep Embed LUT File → On (it's forced Off on SDHC cards, so use an SDXC or CFexpress card).
items.correction: S-Log3 is enabled HERE (Log Shooting → On), not by choosing a gamma in Picture Profile. This body has no Cine EI mode — only Flexible ISO (Cine EI is the FX line / a7S III).

items.name: Select LUT
items.why: Once Log Shooting is On, this picks the LUT you MONITOR through — it only changes the on-screen preview, never the flat S-Log3 you actually record. s709 is the natural, film-like reference and the factory default. You can import your own .cube show LUTs into User1–16.
items.example: Recording S-Log3, s709 in this slot lets the screen show a realistic image while the file stays flat and fully gradeable.
items.alt: 709(800%) for a more broadcast Rec.709 contrast, or a User LUT to preview your target look right on set.

items.name: Display LUT
items.why: The toggle that actually shows your Select LUT on the monitor/finder while you shoot and play back S-Log3 — so flat Log doesn't look broken on screen. On is the factory default. Path: Setup → Display Option → Display LUT. Assign [Display LUT Switch] to a custom key to flip between the graded preview and the raw Log.
items.example: Exposing S-Log3 a stop or two bright for dynamic range, the s709 preview still looks natural so you can judge framing and exposure.
items.alt: Off — if you'd rather judge true Log brightness with a histogram/zebra. Note: the LUT, zebra and peaking are NOT sent over HDMI.
items.correction: Display LUT lives under Setup → Display Option (a monitor preview) — not inside Log Shooting Setting.

items.name: Picture Profile
items.value: PP11 (S-Cinetone) — only if you're NOT shooting Log
items.why: This sets your look when you are NOT shooting Log. If you grade in post you're using Log Shooting (above) — leave Picture Profile alone, because turning Log on drives the picture and overrides these profiles. Reach for PP11 (S-Cinetone) only for same-day, no-grade delivery: it looks finished straight out of camera. The factory default is Off.
items.example: A wedding recap you hand off the next morning — PP11 looks cinematic with zero grading. For anything you'll colour in post, shoot S-Log3 via Log Shooting instead.
items.alt: Off if you'd rather use Creative Looks for your non-Log delivery footage.
items.correction: To shoot S-Log3 you flip Log Shooting → On (Flexible ISO) — you do NOT set an 'S-Log3 gamma' via the PP-LUT slots (those apply LUTs, a different thing).

items.name: Gamma Display Assist
items.value: On (for HLG / S-Log3 monitoring without a LUT)
items.why: Reproduces normal-looking contrast on the monitor so flat S-Log3 or low-contrast HLG footage is easy to judge while shooting and on playback. The factory default is Off. Note it’s locked Off when Log Shooting is On — for S-Log3 you instead monitor with the Display LUT, so this mainly helps HLG.
items.example: Shooting HLG stills or S-Log3 with no custom LUT loaded — Gamma Display Assist makes the dim, washed-out preview look like a natural image.
items.alt: Use the Display LUT (s709) instead for S-Log3 if you want a specific monitoring look rather than a generic 709 conversion.

items.name: Focus Map
items.why: A MOVIE focus aid: while recording, it overlays a colour map of depth of field — cool-coloured dots for areas behind the focus plane, warm dots for areas in front, and clear where the image is sharp. It lets you see your focus plane and pull focus precisely. The dots are never recorded. Factory default is Off. (It only appears in movie mode — stills don't show it.)
items.example: Racking focus from a foreground poppy to a distant ridge in a landscape clip — Focus Map shows exactly when each plane drops into the clear, in-focus band.
items.alt: Peaking Display is the complement (it outlines sharp edges) and works in stills too — use either or both. Note Focus Map needs a native phase-detect lens; it's hidden with adapted lenses, a mount adaptor, Focus Magnifier, or digital zoom.

groups.subtitle: Shooting S-Log3 to grade in post? Start at the top.

items.name: SteadyShot (movie)
items.why: Stabilises movie footage. Standard is light; Active is stronger (narrower angle); Dynamic active is strongest (crops most). On a tripod, turn it OFF — Sony warns it can malfunction against a locked-down body. The factory default is Standard.
items.example: Walking handheld through a forest, Dynamic active smooths your gait; on a tripod for a 4-hour nightlapse, fully Off.
items.alt: Dynamic active for walking shots (max stabilisation, biggest crop). Note Dynamic active is unavailable at 120p+ and disables Clear Image Zoom.

items.name: Auto Slow Shutter
items.why: In dim scenes, automatically lowers the shutter speed to brighten the image — at the cost of more motion blur and judder. Off keeps your shutter at the 180° value you chose (e.g. 1/50 at 24p). The factory default is On; note it’s bypassed the moment you take manual control of shutter/ISO anyway.
items.example: A controlled dusk landscape clip — Off keeps motion crisp at a fixed 1/50; for a casual dim indoor grab where brightness beats cadence, On brightens it for you.
items.alt: On — only for casual run-and-gun in low light where you’d rather the camera keep the image bright than hold a strict shutter cadence.

items.name: Audio Rec Level
items.why: Sets the recording gain for the internal or analog mic while you watch the level meter. Ride it down so the loudest moments peak around −12 to −6 dB rather than clipping. The factory default is 26.
items.example: Filming an interview on a windy Mt. Tam ridge, you drop the level a few notches so gusts and raised voices don't clip into distortion.
items.alt: Leave it auto-ish at the default 26 for run-and-gun, then fix levels in post — but a quick manual set is safer for one-take audio.
items.note: Wind filtering is a separate item in the same Audio Recording group: [Audio NR Settings] → [Wind NR: Int Mic] (default Auto, which detects gusts) — set [Wind NR: Ext Mic] → On for an external mic. No audio records at all in S&Q or time-lapse.

items.name: Proxy Recording
items.value: Off (On if you want phone-fast edits)
items.why: Records a small low-bitrate (≤16 Mbps) companion file alongside the main clip for fast review/transfer. Off is the factory default; your M4 Max edits 4K natively, so you usually don’t need it.
items.example: Recording S-Log3 XAVC S-I 4K masters but wanting to review/post clips from your phone on location — On gives you a tiny HD proxy to share.
items.alt: On if you cut on a laptop or offload to a phone. Caveat: proxy is unavailable at 120p/100p and can lower the main file’s max bitrate.

stages.title: Autofocus
stages.subtitle: Layer 3 · global defaults; presets refine
stages.blurb: The AF defaults you want when no preset is loaded. Recognition target and the AF-C tuning sliders are then overridden per scenario. AF is stored separately for Still and Movie.

gate.headline: Stills AF defaults
gate.detail: AF is stored separately for Still and Movie. We’re setting the Still defaults.

items.name: Focus Mode
items.why: Decides how the camera drives focus. Continuous AF refocuses constantly while you hold AF-ON/half-press — the right default for everything except tripod/manual work. Single-shot locks once; DMF lets you fine-tune by hand after AF; MF is full manual. (Sony’s “Continuous AF” is what most call AF-C.)
items.example: A red-tailed hawk peels off a thermal — Continuous AF keeps refocusing as it dives toward you; Single-shot would lock at the launch distance and miss.
items.alt: Single-shot AF for static landscape detail; Manual Focus for astro and focus-stacking. The body’s factory default is actually Automatic AF (it auto-switches) — we prefer explicit Continuous AF so the camera never decides to lock.

items.name: Focus Area
items.why: Defines where on the frame AF looks. Wide uses the whole sensor and lets subject recognition do the work — the neutral default. Options: Wide / Zone / Center Fix / Spot XL-L-M-S-XS / Expand Spot / Custom 1–3 / Tracking variants. Presets choose Spot/Zone/Tracking.
items.example: A lone egret on clean sky — Wide + Bird recognition just locks the eye; but in cluttered reeds you’d switch to Tracking: Zone so AF doesn’t grab the background.
items.alt: Zone or Tracking: Spot for birds-in-flight; Spot M/L or Expand Spot for perched subjects and precise landscape detail.

items.name: Peaking Display
items.why: Outlines the highest-contrast (sharpest) edges in manual/DMF focus with a coloured overlay, so you can confirm focus by eye. [Peaking Level] sets sensitivity and [Peaking Color] the hue. Factory default is Off / Mid / White; Red stands out far better against foliage and sky.
items.example: Manually focusing on the Milky Way — red peaking lights up the brightest stars exactly when they snap to their sharpest point.
items.alt: White (default) is fine on dark scenes; switch to Red or Yellow against green or bright backgrounds. On low-contrast stars, confirm with focus magnification too.

items.name: Subject Recog in AF
items.why: The master switch for the AI engine. When On, AF prioritises a recognised subject’s eye/head/body inside or near the focus area. On/Off; presets pick the target class.
items.example: A coyote trots across a Marin hillside — with recognition On the box snaps to its eye instead of the grass it’s standing in.
items.alt: Off — only for astro/landscape MF, or when false detections (a rock read as an animal) are worse than none. Assign [Recognition Off on Hold] to a key to suspend it momentarily instead.

items.name: Recognition Target
items.why: Tells the AI which subject class to find. Recognition is class-specific — the wrong target weakens eye lock — so each scenario preset forces a specific one. Auto is the convenient global default that picks from your checked targets. Targets: Auto / Human / Animal/Bird / Animal / Bird / Insect / Car/Train / Airplane.
items.example: Switching from the factory default (Human) to Bird before a heron sequence is exactly what lets the camera find the bird’s eye instead of its body.
items.alt: Animal/Bird (combined) for safaris/mixed scenes so you don’t menu-dive; it adds a Recognition Priority Set. Slightly less precise than a single dedicated class. Factory default is Human.

items.name: Recog Trgt Select Set
items.why: Defines the short list of recognition targets you cycle through with a custom key assigned to [Recog. Target Select]. Check only the classes you actually shoot so one button press flips Bird → Animal → Human without ever opening the menu.
items.example: A bobcat appears mid bird-walk — one press of your Recog. Target Select key jumps from Bird to Animal and the eye box re-acquires, instead of fumbling through the full menu.
items.alt: Leave it unset if you only ever shoot one subject class; then the full Recognition Target menu is all you need.

items.name: Recognition Sensitivity
items.why: How aggressively the AI declares a subject "recognised", on a 1 (Low) to 5 (High) scale. High catches distant, small or partly-hidden subjects and grabs the initial lock fast; low avoids false positives where the camera keeps "recognising" rocks or branches. It lives inside each target’s detailed settings (Animal/Bird/Insect/Vehicle).
items.example: A speck of an eagle against bright cloud — Recognition Sensitivity 5 gets the eye/body lock a lower value would never find.
items.alt: 1–2 (Low) in a busy scene where the camera keeps falsely locking onto non-subjects.

items.name: Tracking Persistence Lvl
items.value: 5 (Locked On) when subjects duck behind cover
items.why: When a recognised subject is briefly lost, this decides whether AF keeps holding the area it was in (5, Locked On) or immediately drops to the nearest other subject (1, Not Locked On). Higher values ride out momentary occlusions instead of jumping away.
items.example: A bird flits behind a tree trunk and back out — at 5 the box stays parked where it was and re-grabs the bird, not the trunk.
items.alt: 1 (Not Locked On) for fast, erratic flyers you want AF to re-acquire instantly wherever they reappear.

items.name: Tracking Shift Range
items.value: 2–3 to stay on one chosen subject
items.why: How far from the tracking frame a different recognised subject can be and still steal focus. Narrow keeps AF on the subject under your box; Wide lets it grab any recognised subject across the frame. Tune it to whether you’re after one animal or whichever one shows up.
items.example: One specific deer among a scattered herd — a narrow shift range keeps AF on the one under your box instead of hopping to the group.
items.alt: 5 (Wide) for flocks or herds where you’ll take whichever recognised subject the camera finds first.

items.name: AF Lvl for Crossing
items.why: This is the body’s stills “tracking sensitivity”: how readily AF leaves the current subject for something that crosses in front. Scale 1 (Locked on) → 5 (Responsive). Lower = sticky (good in clutter); higher = grabby (good for erratic birds). Set per preset.
items.example: A swallow darts over a pond at 5 — AF re-grabs it instantly as it crosses; a kingfisher behind reeds at 1 — focus ignores the foreground stalks.
items.alt: 5 (Responsive) for birds-in-flight and approaching subjects; 1 (Locked on) in branchy, reedy habitat.
items.correction: There is no setting literally named “AF Tracking Sensitivity” on this body — this (AF Lvl for Crossing, 1–5) is it.

items.name: AF Trk for Spd Chng
items.why: How aggressively AF reacts when the subject’s speed suddenly changes. Responsive holds focus through abrupt starts/stops; Stable favours steady or motionless subjects and helps in low light. Sony recommends starting at Standard and only moving it if AF feels unstable.
items.example: A perched eagle suddenly bursts into flight — Responsive keeps focus locked through the sharp acceleration.
items.alt: Responsive for subjects that launch/stop unpredictably; Stable if AF feels jittery in dark scenes or on a slow, constant-speed glider.

items.name: Priority Set in AF-C
items.why: Decides whether the shutter is allowed to fire when focus isn’t confirmed during Continuous AF. [AF] refuses to fire until it’s sharp — far fewer soft frames in a burst. Options: AF / Release / Balanced Emphasis. The factory default is Balanced Emphasis.
items.example: A 20 fps burst of a diving osprey — set to AF, the camera skips the unfocused frames so your burst isn’t half soft.
items.alt: Release — only if you’d rather have a slightly-soft frame than miss a once-in-a-lifetime moment entirely. Most wildlife shooters change this away from the default first.

items.name: AF w/ Shutter
items.why: Whether a half-press of the shutter drives autofocus. Setting it Off is half of back-button focus — focus is driven only by the [AF On] button, so a recompose never re-racks focus. The factory default is On. On/Off.
items.example: Back-button setup — hold AF-ON to track a flying gull, then tap the shutter purely to fire; the half-press never steals focus back to the background.
items.alt: On — simpler for casual landscape/travel where you want one button to both focus and shoot.

items.name: Full Time DMF
items.why: Lets you grab the focus ring and fine-tune at any time during autofocus, without switching to MF mode. The factory default is Off. On/Off.
items.example: AF locks onto foreground grass instead of a half-hidden deer — a quick twist of the ring pulls focus to the deer without leaving Continuous AF.
items.alt: Off — if you bump the focus ring by accident, or use a lens that doesn’t support it (it’s unavailable during continuous shooting in Continuous/Automatic AF).

items.name: Priority Set in AF-S
items.why: The Single-shot-AF twin of Priority Set in AF-C: whether the shutter may fire before focus is confirmed. [AF] won’t release until the shot is genuinely sharp — the right call for deliberate landscape and detail work. The factory default is Balanced Emphasis. Options: AF / Release / Balanced Emphasis.
items.example: A precise lock on a distant lighthouse — on AF priority the camera refuses to fire until it’s actually sharp, so you never bring home a soft "keeper".
items.alt: Release for candid grab-shots where timing matters more than critical sharpness.

stages.title: Custom Buttons & Dials
stages.subtitle: Layer 4 · make the deep menu disappear
stages.blurb: Configure under Setup › Operation Customize › Custom Key/Dial Set. The map is separate for Still, Movie, and Playback — so you build each layout independently. (Function names below are exactly as Sony lists them.)

groups.title: Stills layout
groups.subtitle: The most-needed control is one press away.

gate.headline: Build the STILLS custom-key map
gate.detail: The Custom Key map is separate per mode. Set the dial to Still so you’re editing the stills layout.
gate.note: Setup › Operation Customize › Custom Key/Dial Set.

items.name: AF-ON
items.why: Focus fires only from this rear thumb button (the heart of back-button focus, once AF w/ Shutter is Off), decoupling it from the shutter. In Continuous AF, HOLD AF-ON to keep focus glued to a moving subject — it tracks and refocuses as the subject moves — and RELEASE to lock focus at that distance, so you can recompose and the shutter never re-racks it. In Single-shot AF (or any mode) the same button just focuses once per press. One thumb does track, hold, and lock.
items.example: A perched heron: hold AF-ON and the box rides its eye as it shifts; the moment it lifts off, keep holding to track the flight — or release to lock the perched-distance frame and fire — and the shutter alone never refocuses on the background.
items.alt: Eye AF / Subject Recognition AF on a held key if you wanted the AF-ON press to also force recognition; prefer that only if you shoot fast erratic subjects and want recognition engaged exactly while the button is down.

items.name: Shutter half-press
items.why: This is the AF w/ Shutter menu setting, not a key: turning it Off severs autofocus from the shutter button so a half-press only meters and arms the shutter. Combined with AF On on the rear button, this is what makes true back-button focus work; without it the camera would re-hunt every time you press the shutter. It is the mandatory companion setting to cc-s-1.
items.example: You have AF-locked a perched owl with AF-ON; as it launches you mash the shutter into a 20fps burst and the focus stays exactly where your thumb placed it instead of jumping to the nearest twig.
items.alt: Leave AF w/ Shutter On only if you abandon back-button focus and want a simpler one-button shooting style for casual hand-offs of the camera.

items.name: C1
items.why: Recog. Target Select cycles through the subject-recognition targets you pre-checked (Bird, Animal, Human, etc.) so the camera knows what kind of eye to lock onto. On the top C1 button it sits under your index finger for an instant Bird-to-Animal swap without diving into menus. For mixed wildlife outings this saves the few seconds that decide whether you get the shot.
items.example: You are photographing shorebirds when a coyote trots across the mudflat; one press of C1 flips recognition from Bird to Animal and the camera grabs the coyote's eye before it disappears into the grass.
items.alt: Subject Recognition on/off toggle if you mostly shoot one subject type and just want to disable recognition for branches-in-the-way situations.

items.name: C2
items.why: Focus Area cycles Spot / Zone / Wide / Tracking so you can match the AF pattern to the subject's behavior. C2 is its factory home and a natural reach for your index finger, letting you tighten to Spot for a static perched bird or open to Wide/Tracking for erratic flight. Switching focus area is one of the most frequent in-the-field decisions for wildlife, so it earns a top-deck button.
items.example: A songbird flits from branch to branch: you tap C2 from Spot to Tracking so the box follows it through the foliage instead of you chasing the point with the joystick.
items.alt: Tracking On/Off toggle if you prefer to keep a single area and just switch tracking in and out; useful for predictable perch-and-launch behavior.

items.name: C3
items.why: Cnt Shoot Speed Boost momentarily jumps the drive to a high electronic-shutter frame rate (e.g. 30fps) only while the key is held, then drops back to your normal rate. On C3 it gives you a burst-on-demand thumb without filling cards during slow moments. This is ideal for conserving buffer and storage until the decisive action actually happens.
items.example: A pelican is gliding slowly so you shoot at a modest rate; the instant it folds its wings to plunge-dive you press and hold C3 to rip 30fps through the dive, then release to stop flooding the card.
items.alt: Drive Mode if you would rather deliberately set and leave a continuous speed; prefer that for sustained action like a flock in flight where you do not want to hold a button.

items.name: C4 (front, near grip)
items.why: APS-C S35 Shooting crops to the Super 35 area for roughly 1.5x extra reach and smaller files, using only the sensor's center. On C4 by the grip your trigger finger can punch in reach the moment a subject is too far for your 200-600 or 70-200. It is a reach multiplier you toggle constantly when the wildlife will not come closer.
items.example: A distant osprey sits at the far end of the lake just out of frame-filling range; you tap C4 to crop to APS-C, instantly gaining the equivalent of ~900mm so the bird fills the frame for the catch.
items.alt: Focus Magnifier on C4 if you shoot more static subjects where confirming critical focus matters more than instant reach.

items.name: AEL button
items.why: Regist. AF Area hold recalls a focus point or zone you registered to a fixed spot in the frame while you hold the button, then returns to your live point on release. On the AEL button it lets you snap focus to a pre-planned location like a known perch or flight lane without moving the joystick. This is a setup-shot tool for when you know where the action will be.
items.example: You have framed a flight lane between two trees and registered an AF zone there; as a hawk enters the gap you hold AEL to instantly jump the focus box to that lane and grab the bird mid-flight.
items.alt: AEL hold (the factory function) if you shoot in manual-meter situations and need to lock exposure off a bright sky more than you need a recalled focus area.

items.name: Center button
items.why: Bright Monitoring boosts live-view gain so you can see and compose in near-total darkness — invaluable for framing astro and nightlapse shots. It is a custom-key-only function (no menu toggle), and the control-wheel centre button is unassigned by default, so it is its natural home.
items.example: Composing the Milky Way over a black ridgeline — press the centre button and the foreground jumps into view long enough to frame, then returns to normal.
items.alt: Leave it as Focus Standard if you'd rather the wheel centre also recenters the AF point — but the joystick press already does that, so Bright Monitoring is the higher-value pick.

items.name: Control-wheel ▲
items.why: DISP cycles the on-screen overlays (info, level, histogram, no-info) so you can call up exactly the data you need for the moment. On the control-wheel up position it stays on its factory home, a quick thumb-flick away. Toggling to the histogram or level is constant for exposure-critical landscape work.
items.example: Setting up a sunrise landscape, you flick the wheel up to bring up the live histogram, confirm the highlights are not clipping, then flick again to a clean view for final composition.
items.alt: No strong alternative; this is the conventional, muscle-memory home for display toggling.

items.name: Control-wheel ◄
items.why: Drive Mode switches Single / Continuous / Bracket / Self-timer so you can change how the shutter fires. On the control-wheel left position it keeps Sony's default location, reachable without looking. Bracketing and the self-timer matter for landscape and astro, while continuous matters for wildlife, so frequent access pays off.
items.example: At a waterfall you flick the wheel left to set a 2-second self-timer, eliminating shutter shake on a tripod long exposure without needing a remote.
items.alt: No strong alternative; Drive Mode is the standard assignment here and pairs logically with the Cnt Shoot Speed Boost on C3.

items.name: Control-wheel ►
items.why: ISO gives direct access to sensitivity, the third leg of exposure. On the control-wheel right position it sits at its factory home for a fast thumb adjustment. For wildlife in changing light and for astro where you push high ISO, quick access keeps you from chasing exposure through menus.
items.example: A bird moves from sunlit water into shaded reeds; you flick the wheel right and bump ISO two stops to hold your shutter speed and freeze the wingbeats in the shade.
items.alt: White Balance if you shoot RAW and would rather not touch ISO here because it already lives on the rear dial R; useful for fine-tuning live-view color in mixed light.

items.name: Control-wheel ▼
items.why: Focus Mode switches Continuous AF / Single-shot AF / DMF / Manual Focus, letting you change the autofocus behavior itself. On the control-wheel down position it is one flick away when the subject type changes. Astro and macro want MF while wildlife wants AF-C, so a body that does both benefits from a quick toggle.
items.example: You finish tracking birds in AF-C, then point at the rising Milky Way; a flick of the wheel down to Manual Focus lets you set infinity by hand for the stars.
items.alt: Exposure Comp. if you prefer dialing brightness here and switch focus modes rarely; better for a shooter who lives mostly in one AF mode.

items.name: Front dial
items.why: Aperture on the front dial is the primary depth-of-field control, falling under your index finger for the most-adjusted setting in aperture-priority and manual. Landscapes live and die by aperture (deep focus vs. diffraction), so the front-dial home keeps it instant. This is the conventional, ergonomic placement for the most frequent exposure input.
items.example: Composing a foreground-flower-to-distant-peak landscape, you roll the front dial to f/11 for front-to-back sharpness without lifting your eye from the finder.
items.alt: No strong alternative; aperture on the front dial is the standard pro layout.

items.name: Rear dial L
items.why: Shutter Speed on the rear dial L is your motion control, set by your thumb for freezing or blurring action in manual mode. Wildlife needs fast speeds to freeze wingbeats while waterfalls need slow ones, so a dedicated dial is essential. Pairing shutter (rear) with aperture (front) is the classic two-dial manual workflow.
items.example: A duck explodes off the water; you spin the rear dial L up to 1/2000s to freeze the spray of droplets in mid-air.
items.alt: No strong alternative; shutter on the rear dial is the standard pro layout.

items.name: Rear dial R
items.why: Exposure Comp / ISO on the rear dial R gives a second thumb-reachable exposure input, here set to compensation by default for quick brightness nudges in semi-auto modes. Backlit birds and snowy or bright-sky scenes constantly need exposure compensation, so a dedicated dial beats a button-plus-dial. You can swap it to ISO if you prefer manual-mode sensitivity here.
items.example: Shooting an egret against bright water, you dial in +1 EV on the rear dial R so the white feathers render bright without going gray from the meter.
items.alt: ISO if you shoot full manual and would rather adjust sensitivity here; prefer that when you let exposure comp ride and need direct ISO instead.

items.name: Multi-selector (joystick)
items.why: The multi-selector moves the focus point directly and its press triggers Focus Standard to recenter. As the joystick this is the primary, tactile way to place AF exactly where you want without taking your eye off the finder. Precise point placement is core to careful wildlife and landscape focusing.
items.example: A warbler perches with its eye in the upper-left third; you flick the joystick to drop the focus point right on the eye, then press AF-ON to lock.
items.alt: No strong alternative; focus-point control is the joystick's intended purpose.

items.name: Movie REC button
items.why: Focus Magnifier punches the live view in to a high magnification for confirming manual focus with pixel-level precision. Repurposing the otherwise-idle Movie REC button in stills mode gives this critical-focus tool a big, dedicated key. For astro, macro, and tripod landscape work where AF cannot be trusted, this verifies sharpness before the shot.
items.example: Focusing on a bright star, you press the REC button to magnify, nudge the focus ring until the star collapses to a tight point, then shoot the night sky knowing it is razor sharp.
items.alt: Bright Monitoring on this button if your night work is more about seeing to compose than confirming focus; choose based on which problem you hit first in the dark.

groups.title: Movie layout
groups.subtitle: A separate map for video work.

gate.headline: Build the MOVIE custom-key map
gate.detail: Flip to Movie — this is a completely separate Custom Key layout.
gate.note: Setup › Operation Customize › Custom Key/Dial Set.

items.name: REC button
items.why: Movie Shooting is the record start/stop trigger, kept on its default REC button so the camera behaves predictably and anyone can roll the camera. Leaving the obvious control on its obvious button avoids fumbling at the start of a take. For a hybrid shooter this consistency between modes matters.
items.example: A bobcat steps into your composed scene; you simply press REC where your thumb expects it and start the clip without hunting for a reassigned key.
items.alt: No strong alternative; record on the REC button is the sensible default.

items.name: AF-ON
items.why: AF On in movie mode lets you trigger autofocus deliberately with your thumb so you can hold focus and pull only when you choose, rather than letting the camera ride focus continuously. On AF-ON this matches your stills muscle memory and keeps focus pulls intentional. Controlled focus is what separates a clean rack from distracting hunting on video.
items.example: While filming a perched hawk you keep focus locked, then as it leans to take off you press AF-ON once to smoothly pull focus onto its new position for the launch.
items.alt: AF/MF toggle hold if you want to drop to full manual focus during a take; prefer that for cinematic hand-pulled racks where you control the ring.

items.name: C1
items.why: Display LUT Switch toggles a viewing LUT (like s709) on the monitor while you actually record flat S-Log3, so you can judge a near-final look without baking it in. On C1 it is a quick thumb toggle between the flat capture view and the graded preview. This makes exposing and framing log footage far more intuitive in the field.
items.example: Filming a sunset in S-Log3 that looks washed out, you tap C1 to apply the s709 LUT and instantly see the warm graded colors, confirming the composition reads before you stop down.
items.alt: Gamma Disp. Assist on/off if you prefer the simpler built-in assist instead of a custom LUT; useful when you have not loaded a user LUT.

items.name: C2
items.why: Focus Magnifier punches in to verify critical focus during standby or recording, essential because log footage on a small monitor hides soft focus. On C2 it gives a fast confirm-focus check before you roll. Missed focus is unrecoverable in video, so a dedicated magnify key earns its place.
items.example: Before rolling on a static landscape timelapse-style shot, you press C2 to magnify the distant ridge, confirm it is tack sharp, then start recording with confidence.
items.alt: Peaking Display toggle if you focus by eye and prefer edge highlighting over a punch-in; better for moving handheld shots where magnification is impractical.

items.name: C3
items.why: Picture Profile jumps between profiles like PP11 (S-Cinetone) and S-Log3 so you can switch your capture pipeline between deliverable types. On C3 it lets you change the entire color/gamma setup between setups without menu diving. A hybrid creator who mixes quick-turnaround and graded projects changes this often.
items.example: You finish a quick social clip in S-Cinetone and move to a hero shot for grading; one trip through C3 switches to S-Log3 so the next take carries maximum dynamic range.
items.alt: Creative Look select if you shoot mostly straight-out-of-camera looks and rarely change gamma; prefer that for fast, non-log delivery.

items.name: C4
items.why: APS-C S35 Shooting pre-sets the Super 35 crop, which is the sensor area used for many high-frame-rate movie modes and gives extra reach. On C4 you can arm the crop before switching into slow-motion work. Setting it ahead of time avoids a mid-shoot scramble when you change frame rates.
items.example: Before a slow-motion clip of a hummingbird at a feeder, you tap C4 to set the S35 crop so the high-frame-rate mode and tighter framing are both ready when you hit record.
items.alt: Focus Magnifier on C4 if you rarely shoot S35 and would rather double up your focus-check keys.

items.name: AEL
items.why: Zebra Display overlays stripes on areas at a set brightness so you can nail exposure fast without a histogram, which is invaluable for run-and-gun log shooting. On AEL it is a quick exposure-sanity toggle for skin or highlights. Blown highlights are hard to recover in video, so instant zebra access protects every take.
items.example: Filming handheld as clouds open up, you tap AEL to flash zebras and immediately see the egret's white feathers tipping into clipping, so you pull exposure before rolling.
items.alt: Gamma Disp. Assist if your exposure judgment depends more on seeing the graded image than on zebra thresholds; useful when shooting log without a LUT loaded.

items.name: Control-wheel ►
items.why: ISO (or White Balance) on the control-wheel right gives direct exposure or color control while recording, since you can adjust these live during a movie take. On the wheel's right position it is a discreet thumb adjustment that does not jar the camera. For changing-light outdoor video, on-the-fly ISO or WB keeps the look consistent.
items.example: A cloud passes and dims your scene mid-clip; you quietly roll in higher ISO on the wheel to hold brightness without stopping the take or visibly shifting the frame.
items.alt: White Balance if you lock ISO via Auto or the dials and care more about holding color across a shot; prefer it for mixed natural and artificial light.

items.name: Front/rear dials
items.why: Aperture and Shutter Speed on the front and rear dials give standard two-dial manual exposure for video, where you keep shutter near 180-degree (e.g. 1/50s at 24p) and use aperture for look and light. Keeping them on the main dials mirrors your stills layout for muscle-memory consistency. Manual control is the norm for serious video to avoid auto-exposure pumping.
items.example: Setting up a daylight clip at 24p, you lock the rear dial to 1/50s for natural motion blur and roll the front dial to f/4 for a soft background, adding an ND if needed.
items.alt: No strong alternative; aperture/shutter on the twin dials is the standard manual-video layout.

items.name: Center button
items.why: Subject Recognition AF on a held center button forces the camera to actively detect and lock a specific subject only while you hold it, overriding ambient AF behavior. This lets you command recognition at the exact instant you want it rather than leaving it always on. For wildlife video it pins the eye on cue without committing recognition for the whole take.
items.example: A fox crosses behind grass and the AF drifts; you press and hold the center button to force subject recognition, the camera grabs its eye, and you hold it sharp until it clears the frame.
items.alt: AF On (hold) if you would rather just command plain autofocus here without recognition; prefer it when recognition false-locks on busy backgrounds.

stages.title: Fn Menu
stages.subtitle: Layer 4 · your 12-tile quick panel
stages.blurb: The Fn menu is your 12-tile quick panel — separate for stills and movie (Setup › Operation Customize › Fn Menu Settings). Populate it with what you change often but didn’t assign to a dedicated button.

gate.headline: Stills Fn panel
gate.detail: The Fn layout is separate per mode. Set Still, then press Fn → customise.

gate.headline: Movie Fn panel
gate.detail: Flip to Movie — a separate 12-tile panel.

stages.title: My Menu
stages.subtitle: Layer 4 · your “deep but important” list
stages.blurb: My Menu (last tab) is your deep-but-important list — settings buried several levels down that you still touch. Add them, then set “Display From My Menu” to On so MENU opens here.

gate.headline: Build My Menu
gate.detail: My Menu › My Menu Setting › Add Item. Order them as below.

items.name: Display From My Menu
items.why: Display From My Menu set to On makes the MENU button open straight to your My Menu tab, putting your hand-picked, frequently-touched settings front and center instead of the default top of the menu tree. It turns a deep, multi-tab menu system into a one-press shortcut list.
items.example: In the field you need to flip Auto Switch Media Card or a Picture Profile fast; pressing MENU lands you directly on your curated list rather than making you hunt through tabs while the light changes.
items.alt: Off — leave it off if you navigate by the full menu tabs and don't want My Menu intercepting every MENU press.

scenario.tagline: Lock a motion-stopping shutter, let ISO float in changing forest light.
scenario.fieldNote: At 30 fps the shutter can’t go slower than 1/30 s, and the Lossless-Comp buffer is ~35 frames (electronic) — burst in short taps. CFexpress Type A clears the buffer faster than a V90 SD. The 200-600 is a native Sony G lens, so SteadyShot reads its focal length and the OSS switch handles panning.

gate.headline: Configure, then save to MR1
gate.detail: Set the body fully in Manual + ISO Auto, then write the entire state into the in-body MR1 register.

items.name: Exposure mode
items.why: Manual + ISO Auto pins your two creative anchors — shutter and aperture — while ISO floats to balance exposure as light shifts. For wildlife you live under a moving canopy: the same coyote walks from open meadow shade into dappled forest in seconds, and letting ISO ride keeps the motion-stopping shutter you dialed locked the whole time. Shutter Priority is the lazier cousin if you don't want to think about aperture.
items.example: A bobcat steps out of the chaparral at Pinnacles into full sun, then ducks back into shadow — ISO swings from 400 to 3200 frame-to-frame while your 1/1000 s and f/6.3 never move, so every shot is sharp and properly exposed.
items.alt: Shutter Priority (S) — same shutter lock with less to manage, useful when you don't care which aperture the camera picks at 600 mm (it's f/6.3 wide open anyway).

items.name: Drive
items.value: Cont. Shooting · Hi+ (10 fps mech); Cnt Shoot Speed Boost → 30 fps on a held key
items.why: Drive sets your frame rate and burst behavior. Mechanical Hi+ at 10 fps is plenty for a static or slow-walking mammal and keeps full dynamic range with zero rolling-shutter risk, while a held custom key punches to 30 fps electronic only for the explosive moment. This two-tier approach saves your buffer and card for action that actually warrants it.
items.example: A tule elk grazes — you tap 10 fps mechanical frames for portraits; the instant a rival charges and they lock antlers, you hold the Speed Boost key and rip 30 fps through the clash.
items.alt: Hi (8 fps mechanical) if your buffer or card is slower and you want longer sustained bursts without filling up.

items.name: Shutter Type
items.why: Shutter Type governs whether the physical curtain or the sensor's electronic readout makes the exposure. Mechanical is your default here: full dynamic range, no banding under artificial light, and no skew on fast-panning subjects. The electronic shutter only takes over during the Speed Boost burst, so you get silence and 30 fps exactly when the action demands it and nowhere else.
items.example: A perched fox sits motionless in even shade — mechanical shutter renders the fur with full tonal range; when it bolts after a ground squirrel, the speed-boost burst silently switches to electronic to catch the sprint.
items.alt: Auto shutter type lets the camera pick mechanical/electronic per situation, but you lose deterministic control over which one fires in a critical moment.

items.name: ISO AUTO Min. SS
items.why: ISO AUTO Min. SS is the floor shutter speed the camera holds before it starts raising ISO. Setting it as an explicit speed (not Standard) guarantees you freeze mammal motion at 600 mm regardless of how dark the forest gets. 1/1000 s handles a walking or standing animal; bump to 1/2000–1/2500 s the moment something starts running.
items.example: A river otter trots along a Point Reyes shoreline in fading light — your 1/2000 s floor forces ISO up to keep the gait crisp instead of letting the shutter sag into motion blur.
items.alt: 1/1600 s as a single compromise floor if you want one setting that covers both ambling and brief sprints without retuning.

items.name: Aperture
items.why: f/6.3 is the maximum aperture of the 200-600 G at the long end, so you're gathering every available photon and getting the cleanest subject-from-background separation. In dim forest light, wide open is almost always the right call — it keeps ISO down and throws the cluttered backdrop into a smooth wash. You'd only stop down if you need more depth across a large or angled subject.
items.example: A black-tailed deer stands against a busy bramble thicket — f/6.3 at 600 mm melts the branches behind it into soft bokeh so the animal pops cleanly off the frame.
items.alt: f/8 when you need a touch more depth of field for a larger mammal at close range or to nudge sharpness, accepting the higher ISO cost.

items.name: Focus Mode / Area
items.why: Continuous AF keeps focus driving as the subject and your framing shift, and Zone gives you a moveable cluster of points that balances easy acquisition with selectivity. For mammals in brush, Zone is the sweet spot — broad enough to grab the animal fast, tight enough not to snap onto foreground leaves. Tracking: Zone hands off to subject recognition once locked.
items.example: A coyote weaves through tall grass — you place the Zone box over its body and AF-C holds the lock as it crosses, ignoring the grass blades whipping through the edges of the frame.
items.alt: Tracking: Spot/Expand Spot for a stationary, clearly visible animal when you want pinpoint placement on the eye and no chance of the box wandering.

items.name: Recognition Target
items.why: Recognition Target tells the AI subject-recognition engine what shape to hunt for. Forcing Animal is more dependable than Auto for mammals because the camera isn't wasting cycles deciding between categories — it commits to finding an animal eye/head/body immediately. That decisiveness matters when a subject appears for only a second.
items.example: A gray fox materializes at the edge of a clearing — with Animal locked in, the green eye box snaps onto it instantly instead of hesitating while Auto rules out human and bird.
items.alt: Animal/Bird if you're in mixed habitat where a raptor might also flush, accepting slightly slower category decisions for broader coverage.

items.name: Right/Left Eye Select
items.why: Right/Left Eye Select set to Auto lets the camera choose whichever eye is visible and, when no eye is clear, gracefully fall back to head then body. For wildlife half-hidden in cover this fallback chain is exactly what you want — partial views still get a usable lock. Note this is the real menu item; there's no standalone Recognition Part menu on the A7 V.
items.example: A bobcat peers through manzanita with only the right side of its face showing — Auto grabs that one visible eye, and when it turns away, the lock slides to the head without dropping.
items.alt: Right Eye (or Left Eye) fixed if you're shooting a predictable profile and want to force focus onto the near eye every frame.

items.name: AF Lvl for Crossing
items.why: AF Lvl for Crossing is the stills tracking-sensitivity slider: how readily focus jumps to a new object that crosses in front of your subject. Scale runs 1 (Locked on) to 5 (Responsive). For mammals in cluttered habitat you want 1–2 so intervening branches and grass blades don't steal focus off the animal you're tracking.
items.example: A deer moves behind a screen of foreground saplings — at AF Lvl 1, focus stays glued to the deer instead of pulling onto each trunk that sweeps through the frame.
items.alt: 3 (Standard) for an animal moving through open terrain where there's little clutter and you want slightly quicker reacquisition.

items.name: Priority Set in AF-C
items.why: Priority Set in AF-C decides whether the camera fires even when focus isn't confirmed (Release) or waits for sharpness (AF/Focus). Setting it to AF means the camera won't waste buffer and your time on soft frames during a burst — every shutter actuation is a confirmed-focus frame. For wildlife where keeper rate beats frame count, that's the right trade.
items.example: A running coyote briefly dips behind a rise — AF priority simply pauses the burst for that fraction of a second rather than machine-gunning blurry frames, then resumes the instant it's sharp again.
items.alt: Balanced Emphasis if you'd rather not miss any moment and can tolerate culling a few soft frames from each burst.

items.name: Pre-Capture
items.why: Pre-Capture continuously buffers frames before you fully press the shutter, so the camera retroactively saves the instant you reacted to too late. It requires electronic shutter plus continuous drive. A ~0.5 s window is enough to claw back the launch or sudden turn that human reaction time always costs you.
items.example: A perched red-tailed hawk drops off its snag — you mash the shutter a beat late, but the 0.5 s pre-roll already captured the wings-spread launch you thought you'd missed.
items.alt: Rec Time 1.0 s for less predictable mammals, at the cost of a fuller buffer and more frames to cull per trigger.

items.name: Metering
items.value: Multi (Spot + Focus Point Link for backlit)
items.why: Metering decides how the camera reads the scene for ISO Auto. Multi evaluates the whole frame and is the reliable default for evenly lit wildlife. The real trick is binding Spot to your focus point (Spot Metering Point → Focus Point Link) so you can meter the animal itself when it's backlit and the surroundings would otherwise fool the exposure.
items.example: A river otter sits rim-lit on a log with bright water behind — switching to focus-linked Spot exposes for the otter's dark fur instead of letting Multi underexpose it into a silhouette.
items.alt: Center-weighted as a simpler middle ground when the subject reliably fills the central portion of the frame.

items.name: SteadyShot
items.why: SteadyShot is in-body stabilization; On is essential when you're handholding a 600 mm lens where every heartbeat is magnified. With a native G lens like the 200-600, the camera reads focal length automatically and the lens's own OSS switch — not a camera menu — handles panning modes. Together they let you track a moving animal smoothly while damping vertical shake.
items.example: You track a trotting tule elk across a meadow — IBIS plus the lens OSS panning mode lets you swing horizontally with the animal while keeping the vertical jitter out of your 600 mm frame.
items.alt: Off only on a locked-down tripod or gimbal, where IBIS can occasionally fight a very stable platform.

items.name: Picture / Look
items.why: This sets file format and color rendering. RAW preserves maximum latitude to recover shadows and highlights in the wildly variable light of forest wildlife, and Creative Look ST is a neutral baseline you grade from later. Note: for sustained 30 fps electronic bursts you should drop to Compressed RAW — Lossless fills the buffer in about a second (~35 frames) versus roughly 95 frames on Compressed, a huge difference when a sprint lasts several seconds.
items.example: A bobcat hunts at dawn in deep shade — RAW lets you lift two stops of shadow in post without falling apart, and Compressed RAW kept the 30 fps buffer alive through the entire pounce.
items.alt: RAW (Compressed) — switch to this whenever you anticipate long 30 fps action so the buffer holds ~95 frames instead of stalling at ~35.

items.name: Save to MR1
items.why: This writes your entire current camera state — exposure mode, drive, AF behavior, recognition, every value above — into the in-body MR1 register in one action. Because it's stored in the body, not on the card, it's instantly recallable, survives card swaps and formatting, and is always there when you twist the mode dial to MR1. This is what turns fourteen menu dives into a single click before the action starts.
items.example: You arrive at a Point Reyes trailhead, rotate to MR1, and your full wildlife rig — 1/1000 floor, Animal recognition, Zone AF, Compressed RAW — is live before the first deer steps out.

scenario.tagline: Fixed fast shutter against bright sky; ISO floats; frame density for the wingbeat.
scenario.fieldNote: Against bright sky, expose for the bird (often +1 EV or spot meter) — the sky will blow, that’s fine. At 30 fps + 1 s pre-capture the buffer fills fast; CFexpress Type A clears it much quicker than a V90 SD. For birds among branches, temporarily lower AF Lvl for Crossing.

gate.headline: Configure, then save to MR2
gate.detail: Manual + ISO Auto, electronic shutter for 30 fps. Then write to in-body MR2.

items.name: Exposure mode
items.why: Manual + ISO Auto locks the fast shutter and aperture you need for flight while ISO floats to handle the bird moving between bright sky and shadowed tree line. Against a bright sky you'll typically dial in roughly +1 EV exposure compensation (it works in M + ISO Auto) so the bird isn't rendered as a dark blob — let the sky blow, that's the correct trade for a properly exposed bird.
items.example: An osprey banks from bright overcast sky down against a dark hillside at the Berkeley Marina — ISO leaps to keep your 1/3200 s shutter pinned through the brightness swing while the bird stays correctly exposed.
