items.alt: Aperture Priority with a high Min SS floor if you'd rather let the camera manage shutter, but you lose the absolute shutter guarantee M gives you.

items.name: Drive
items.value: Cont. Shooting · Hi+ · Cont. Shooting Speed 30 fps (electronic)
items.why: Drive at Cont. Shooting Hi+ with 30 fps electronic gives you the frame density that birds-in-flight demand — wingbeats happen faster than you can anticipate, and 30 fps means the one frame with the wings at full extension is in the sequence. The electronic shutter is mandatory to hit 30 fps. Pair this with Compressed RAW so the buffer holds ~95 frames instead of stalling around 35 on Lossless.
items.example: A great egret lifts off the Palo Alto Baylands mudflat — at 30 fps you bank the entire takeoff sequence, and the single frame with wings arched and feet just clearing the water is the keeper.
items.alt: Hi 20 fps if you want to conserve buffer and card on more predictable, slower flyers while still freezing the wingbeat.

items.name: Shutter Type
items.why: Electronic Shutter is required for 30 fps and gives you silent shooting plus speeds to 1/16000 s. The trade-offs to know: slightly reduced dynamic range, possible banding under LED/artificial light, and the burst shutter can't go slower than 1/30 s. For BIF against open sky none of those bite — you're at fast speeds in natural light, exactly where electronic shines.
items.example: A peregrine stoops over the Marin Headlands — the silent electronic shutter rips 30 fps at 1/5000 s without a single mechanical vibration to soften the dive frames.
items.alt: Auto shutter type if you also shoot perched birds in the same outing and want mechanical to engage automatically for static, full-DR portraits.

items.name: ISO AUTO Min. SS
items.why: ISO AUTO Min. SS is the shutter floor the camera defends before raising ISO. For flight you want it set explicitly to 1/2500–1/4000 s — that's the range that crisply freezes a wingtip at full beat. Anything slower and the fastest part of the stroke smears even when the body looks sharp.
items.example: A forster's tern hovers then plunges for a fish — your 1/4000 s floor freezes the spray and the individual primary feathers instead of letting the wingtips blur into ghosts.
items.alt: 1/2000 s for larger, slower-flapping birds like herons or pelicans where you want lower ISO and the wingbeat is gentler.

items.name: Aperture
items.why: f/6.3 is the 200-600 wide open at 600 mm, maximizing reach and light so ISO stays manageable under the fast shutters BIF requires. Wide open also cleanly separates the bird from a busy background when it drops below the skyline. There's rarely a reason to stop down for a single bird in flight.
items.example: A white-tailed kite hovers over a Coyote Hills grassland — f/6.3 keeps ISO in check at 1/3200 s while softening the dry hillside behind it so the bird stands out sharply.
items.alt: f/8 for a tight formation or two birds at slightly different distances when you need both inside the depth of field.

items.name: Focus Mode / Area
items.value: Continuous AF / Tracking: Zone (perched); Wide or Zone for BIF acquisition
items.why: Continuous AF keeps focus driving on an erratically moving bird. Tracking: Zone works for perched birds where you want a confined, selective box, but for acquiring a bird against open sky, Wide or Zone reacquire far better than Spot — they give the recognition engine room to find and grab a fast, small target. Once locked, bird-eye recognition takes over inside that area.
items.example: A northern harrier quarters low and fast over the marsh — Wide area lets the camera snatch focus the instant it crosses into frame, where a tight Spot box would have missed it entirely.
items.alt: Tracking: Spot for a perched, stationary bird when you want to place the box precisely on the eye with no wandering.

items.name: Recognition Target
items.why: Recognition Target set to Bird points the AI engine specifically at avian shapes and eyes, which is dramatically more reliable than Auto for both perched birds and distant BIF. Committing to Bird means the eye box finds and holds a tiny, fast subject without the camera second-guessing the category. This is the single most impactful AF setting for bird work.
items.example: A distant osprey is just a speck against cloud — with Bird locked, the camera still recognizes it and drives focus onto the body, then snaps to the eye as it fills the frame.
items.alt: Animal/Bird if you might also encounter a mammal on the same outing, accepting marginally slower bird acquisition.

items.name: Right/Left Eye Select
items.why: Right/Left Eye Select on Auto picks whichever eye is visible for a perched bird and falls back to head then body for distant or angled flight subjects. That fallback chain is critical for BIF, where the eye is often unresolvable at distance — you still get a head/body lock that holds the bird sharp. This is the actual menu item; there's no separate Recognition Part control.
items.example: A perched red-shouldered hawk gives you a clean profile — Auto nails the near eye; when it launches and turns away, the lock rides the head through the whole departure.
items.alt: Right or Left Eye fixed for a cooperative perched bird presenting a consistent profile, forcing the near eye every frame.

items.name: AF Lvl for Crossing
items.why: AF Lvl for Crossing is the stills tracking-sensitivity slider, 1 (Locked on) to 5 (Responsive). For erratic birds-in-flight against open sky you want 4–5 so focus reacquires instantly when the bird jukes or briefly leaves the box. The field note's caveat: when shooting birds among branches, temporarily drop this lower so twigs don't steal focus.
items.example: A swallow snaps through unpredictable direction changes over a pond — at AF Lvl 5 the camera re-grabs it after every sharp turn instead of lagging a frame behind.
items.alt: 2–3 when a bird is perched or flying among branches, so foreground clutter doesn't pull focus off it.

items.name: AF Trk for Spd Chng
items.why: AF Trk for Spd Chng controls how aggressively the focus system adapts when the subject's speed suddenly changes. Set to Responsive, it keeps up with a bird that goes from a glide to an explosive flap or a dive without hesitation. Birds rarely move at constant velocity, so this matched to Responsive prevents the focus from coasting and falling behind.
items.example: A pelican glides on a thermal then snaps into a powered dive — Responsive tracking instantly tightens onto the acceleration instead of lagging while it figures out the new speed.
items.alt: Standard for steadier, soaring flight (a circling vulture or kite) where abrupt speed changes are rare and you want smoother, less jumpy tracking.

items.name: Priority Set in AF-C
items.value: AF (or Balanced Emphasis for fast erratic)
items.why: Priority Set in AF-C decides whether the camera fires on every press or only on confirmed focus. AF (Focus) maximizes keeper sharpness by refusing soft frames. If you find you're losing too many frames to focus lag on a fast erratic flyer, Balanced Emphasis is the escape hatch — it lets a few questionable frames through so you don't miss the peak moment entirely.
items.example: A diving osprey accelerates faster than focus can fully confirm — on Balanced Emphasis you keep firing through the plunge and recover several sharp frames you'd have lost waiting on strict AF priority.
items.alt: Balanced Emphasis for the fastest, most erratic flight when missing the moment costs more than culling a few soft frames.

items.name: Pre-Capture
items.why: Pre-Capture buffers up to a full second of frames before you fully press, so a takeoff you physically can't react to in time is still recorded. It needs electronic shutter and continuous drive. Because the launch is the hardest moment to time, the 1.0 s window is set to its maximum here — and that's exactly why your Min SS floor must already be fast (1/2500–1/4000), since the pre-roll fires at your current settings.
items.example: A great blue heron crouches, then explodes off the bank — you trigger a half-second late, but the 1 s pre-roll already banked the wings-spread launch with feet still on the mud.
items.alt: Rec Time 0.5 s if the longer pre-roll is overfilling your buffer and you can react closer to the takeoff.

items.name: SteadyShot
items.why: SteadyShot On stabilizes the inevitable shake of handholding 600 mm on a moving target. With the native 200-600 the body reads focal length automatically and the lens OSS switch — not a camera menu — provides the panning mode that lets you swing horizontally with a bird while damping vertical jitter. This combination is what makes smooth flight pans possible handheld.
items.example: You track a harrier gliding low across the marsh — IBIS plus the lens panning mode lets you sweep with it laterally while killing the up-down bounce in your 600 mm view.
items.alt: Off only on a gimbal or solid tripod where the platform alone provides stability and IBIS could occasionally counteract a deliberate pan.

items.name: Save to MR2
items.why: This writes the complete configuration — 30 fps electronic, 1 s pre-capture, Bird recognition, fast Min SS floor, Responsive tracking — into the in-body MR2 register in a single action. Stored in the body rather than on a card, it recalls instantly the moment you turn the dial to MR2 and survives card swaps and formatting. Reserve these limited in-body slots for exactly this kind of fast, can't-miss scenario.
items.example: A raptor appears overhead at the Marin Headlands hawk watch — you flick to MR2 and the entire BIF rig is armed before it completes its first pass, no menu diving while the bird is in frame.

scenario.tagline: Control DOF on a tripod; time isn’t critical, image quality is everything.
scenario.fieldNote: NiSi Swift: CPL on the outside, ND inside. With ND stacked, AF struggles — use Single-shot AF (contrast-assisted) or MF + Focus Magnifier. Turn [e-Front Curtain Shut.] On and use a 2-sec self-timer or remote to kill shutter shock. Re-check CPL rotation after recomposing.

gate.headline: Configure, then save to M1
gate.detail: M-registers live on the card. Configure on tripod (A or M), then save to M1 — and write the register to both cards.

dials.label: Mode dial → A (Aperture Priority) or M

gate.note: Card registers are erased if you format that card — save to both cards.

items.name: Exposure mode
items.why: Exposure mode sets which exposure variables you control versus the camera. On a stable tripod with no moving subject, you want to lock the creative variable that matters most: in Aperture Priority you dial in depth of field and let the camera pick the shutter speed, while Manual lets you nail both for repeatable bracket frames. Either way you keep ISO at base and let time stretch, since the tripod removes any shake penalty.
items.example: For a high-DR sunrise over the Marin Headlands you set A mode at f/11, let the meter pick the shutter, then chimp the histogram and add exposure comp to push it right.
items.alt: Manual mode — preferred when you bracket for HDR so every frame holds identical aperture and the camera only steps shutter speed, keeping the merge clean.

items.name: Drive
items.value: Single Shooting; Cont. Bracket for AEB; Focus Bracket for stacking
items.why: Drive mode controls how many frames fire per shutter press and in what pattern. Single Shooting is the default for a careful one-frame composition; Cont. Bracket fires an exposure bracket in a burst for HDR, and Focus Bracket walks focus across the scene for stacking. Pairing the bracket drives with a remote or self-timer keeps the tripod dead still through the sequence.
items.example: At Point Reyes you switch to Focus Bracket to march from the foreground lupines to the distant lighthouse, then merge the 12 frames in Photoshop for front-to-back sharpness.
items.alt: Single Shooting — when the scene is a single hyperfocal frame and one well-placed exposure already covers the dynamic range.

items.name: Shutter Type
items.value: Mechanical Shutter + e-Front Curtain Shut. On
items.why: Shutter Type picks the mechanism that ends the exposure. Mechanical Shutter carries the sensor's full dynamic range and is required for Long Exposure NR, e-Front Curtain, and anti-flicker — the electronic shutter disables all of those and trades away DR and bit depth. e-Front Curtain Shutter is a separate On/Off item that opens electronically and closes mechanically, killing the start-of-exposure shutter shock without the readout penalties of full e-shutter.
items.example: For a 4-second smooth-water frame at Rodeo Beach, Mechanical + EFCS On gives you the full tonal range in the wet sand shadows with zero shock blur.

items.name: ISO
items.why: ISO sets sensor amplification. This body has dual base ISO at 100 and 1000; ISO 100 is the cleanest base with the widest exposure latitude, which is exactly what you want when time is free on a tripod. Lower ISO means more headroom to recover shadows and highlights from a single RAW.
items.example: At base ISO 100 your 1/4 s f/11 Marin Headlands frame holds the deepest shadow detail in the headland cliffs without lifting noise in post.
items.alt: ISO 1000 — the second clean base; useful if you need to shorten a windy-grass exposure to freeze motion without jumping to a noisy intermediate value.

items.name: Aperture
items.why: Aperture controls depth of field and, through the lens's MTF, peak sharpness. f/8–f/11 sits in the sweet spot for the Sigma 24-70 II and 70-200 GM II, deep enough for front-to-back focus on most scenes while staying clear of the f/16+ diffraction that softens 33 MP files. Stopping down further buys depth but costs resolution you can't get back.
items.example: f/9 at 24 mm gives a sharp hyperfocal frame at Point Reyes from the foreground rocks to the horizon without diffraction smearing the fine grass texture.
items.alt: f/16 — only when a single frame must cover extreme near-far depth and you'd rather accept slight diffraction than focus-stack.

items.name: Focus Mode / Area
items.value: Single-shot AF / Spot, or Manual Focus + Focus Magnifier
items.why: Focus Mode and Area decide how and where focus locks. Single-shot AF with a Spot area lets you place focus precisely on a static scene and have it stay put, while Manual Focus with Focus Magnifier is the reliable choice when stacked NiSi ND filters starve the AF of contrast. MF also fixes your starting plane for a focus-bracket run.
items.example: With the NiSi Swift CPL+ND stacked at the Marin Headlands, AF hunts, so you switch to MF, magnify on the distant ridge, and set the hyperfocal point by hand.
items.alt: Single-shot AF / Spot — fast and accurate when light is plentiful and no dark ND is dimming the finder.

items.name: Subject Recog in AF
items.why: Subject Recognition in AF tells the camera to hunt for and prioritize recognized subjects like faces, animals, or vehicles. Landscapes have no such subject, so leaving it on risks the system false-locking onto a foreground rock or bird instead of your chosen plane. Turning it off keeps focus exactly where you place it.
items.example: Shooting a Point Reyes seascape, Off stops the camera from grabbing onto a passing gull when you meant to hold focus on the hyperfocal foreground.

items.name: Metering
items.why: Metering chooses how the camera evaluates scene brightness for its exposure suggestion. Multi-segment metering averages the whole frame for a sensible starting point, but in high-DR scenes you verify with the live histogram and Zebra Display to expose to the right without clipping highlights. The histogram, not the meter, is your real exposure tool here.
items.example: On a high-DR Marin Headlands sunrise you push exposure until the zebras just kiss the bright sky, then back off a third stop to hold the sun's edge.
items.alt: Spot metering — when you want to meter a specific midtone, like the lit cliff face, and build exposure around it.

items.name: Bracket Settings
items.value: AEB 3–5 frames; or Focus Bracket (Step Width 3–5, 10–20 shots, order 0→+)
items.why: Bracket Settings define how exposure or focus brackets step. For HDR you set AEB to 3–5 frames at 1–2 EV spacing to span the scene's range; for focus stacking you set Step Width and shot count so depth of field overlaps frame to frame. Critically, the camera does not merge either bracket in-body — you stack in Lightroom or Photoshop.
items.example: For a high-DR Marin sunrise, a 5-frame AEB at 2 EV captures the bright sun and shadowed headland, then merges to HDR in Lightroom.
items.alt: Focus Bracket (Step Width 3–5, 10–20 shots, order 0→+) — for a deep near-far Point Reyes scene where one aperture can't hold both planes sharp.

items.name: SteadyShot
items.why: SteadyShot is the in-body image stabilization that floats the sensor to counter handheld shake. On a tripod there is no shake to cancel, and the active IBIS can actually hunt and introduce a slow drift blur during long exposures. Turning it off locks the sensor for the sharpest possible tripod frames.
items.example: For a 4-second long exposure of smoothed surf at Rodeo Beach, SteadyShot Off keeps the static rocks pin-sharp instead of softly smeared by IBIS micro-motion.

items.name: Long Exposure NR
items.value: On for single >1 s frames; Off when bracketing/stacking
items.why: Long Exposure NR shoots a matching dark frame after each exposure of 1 s or longer and subtracts it, removing hot pixels and thermal noise — but it doubles the total capture time and requires Mechanical Shutter. It's worth it for a one-off ultra-long single frame, but for bracketing or stacking you turn it off (and the camera disables it automatically during continuous/bracket drive).
items.example: For a single 30-second exposure of a calm Point Reyes lagoon, On cleans up the hot pixels in the dark water; during a 5-frame HDR bracket you leave it Off.
items.alt: Off — the right choice whenever you're firing a bracket or any stacked sequence, so frames aren't interrupted by dark-frame processing.

items.name: Picture / Look
items.why: Picture/Look sets the file format and baseline color rendering. RAW in Lossless Compressed captures the full sensor data with no Uncompressed penalty (this body has no Uncompressed option) and gives you maximum latitude to grade highlights and shadows. Creative Look ST is the neutral starting profile; with RAW it only affects the preview, since you finish in Lightroom.
items.example: Your high-DR Marin sunrise RAW in Lossless Comp lets you pull two stops of shadow out of the headland cliffs in Lightroom without banding.
items.alt: RAW + JPEG — add a JPEG if you want quick previews to cull in the field before processing the RAWs.

items.name: Save to M1 (both cards)
items.why: This writes the entire landscape configuration into the M1 camera-setting memory register so a single dial turn recalls aperture, drive, shutter type, ISO, and the rest. M1 is one of the card-based registers, so saving it stores the recipe on the memory card. Save it to both cards so swapping cards in the field doesn't lose your landscape preset.
items.example: After dialing in f/11, base ISO, Mechanical+EFCS and Single drive at the trailhead, you write it to M1 so the next Marin sunrise is one dial flick away.

stages.label: C4
stages.title: Astrophotography
stages.subtitle: MR3 · in-body · Milky Way, stars, stacking

scenario.tagline: Full manual control — the meter is useless in the dark. Chase photons, protect the stars.
scenario.fieldNote: Use [Bright Monitoring] (assign to Fn) to compose in the dark. For exposures past 30 s, [BULB Timer Settings] gives a timed BULB (2–900 s). For foreground + sky, shoot two exposures and blend; stack 10–20 frames in Sequator and denoise in Lightroom. ([Composite RAW Sht.] can also shoot 4/8/16 RAWs for you, but it’s merged on computer, not in-camera.)

gate.headline: Configure, then save to MR3
gate.detail: Full Manual, MF to infinity via Focus Magnifier. Then write to the in-body MR3 register.

items.name: Exposure mode
items.why: Exposure mode sets which variables you control. In the dark the meter has nothing useful to read, so Manual is mandatory — you set aperture, shutter, and ISO yourself from known astro starting points rather than trusting any automatic exposure. This also keeps every frame in a stack identical for clean integration.
items.example: For a 20-second Milky Way frame over Mount Tamalpais you set M mode f/2.8, 20 s, ISO 1000 by hand because the meter sees only black.

items.name: Drive
items.value: Single Shooting (or Cont. Shooting for stacking sets)
items.why: Drive mode sets how frames fire. Single Shooting suits a deliberate single exposure or a foreground frame, while Cont. Shooting rattles off the 10–20 light frames you'll later integrate in Sequator to beat down noise. Note that continuous drive disables Long Exposure NR, which is exactly what you want for an uninterrupted stack.
items.note: Older Sony bodies applied spatial NR (“star-eater”) to long exposures that dimmed faint stars. Sony’s manual says nothing about it — bracket a test and inspect before trusting long single frames.
items.example: For a Milky Way stack at Point Reyes you switch to Cont. Shooting and fire 15 back-to-back 20 s frames to average out noise in Sequator.
items.alt: Single Shooting — for a one-off blended foreground exposure or a single hero frame where you control each release.

items.name: Shutter Type
items.why: Shutter Type picks the exposure mechanism. Mechanical Shutter carries the full dynamic range and avoids the electronic shutter's readout artifacts and DR loss, which matters when you're lifting faint Milky Way detail. e-Front Curtain On removes start-of-exposure shock without the e-shutter penalties, and Mechanical is also required if you ever enable Long Exposure NR for a single frame.
items.example: For a 20-second Milky Way frame, Mechanical + EFCS preserves the faint shadow gradient of the galactic core that e-shutter would flatten.

items.name: ISO
items.why: ISO sets amplification, and this sensor's dual base design puts a second clean native floor at ISO 1000 — the sweet spot for the Milky Way, giving a bright signal with minimal read noise. You can push to 3200–6400 for a brighter live preview and more signal per frame, then denoise and stack in post; going higher mainly trades highlight headroom for a brighter LCD.
items.example: At ISO 1000, f/2.8, 20 s the galactic core renders bright and clean over the Marin Headlands, sitting right on the second base ISO for the lowest noise.
items.alt: ISO 3200 — brightens the histogram and live view for easier composition when stacking will clean up the extra noise anyway.

items.name: Aperture
items.why: Aperture controls how much light hits the sensor — the single biggest factor in astro. Wide open at f/2.8 the Sigma 24-70 II gathers four times the light of f/5.6, letting you keep ISO and shutter reasonable while the stars stay bright. The 24 mm end is your widest fast field of view in the kit, ideal for sweeping Milky Way arches.
items.example: f/2.8 at 24 mm over Point Reyes pulls in enough photons to render the dust lanes of the core in a single 20 s frame.
items.alt: f/3.2 — stop down a third of a stop if coma or astigmatism smears the corner stars and you want tighter points.

items.name: Shutter time
items.value: NPF / “300 rule”: ~12 s at 24 mm; ~8–10 s if pixel-peeping
items.why: Shutter time balances signal against star trailing — too long and the Earth's rotation streaks the stars. The NPF/300 rule gives about 12 s at 24 mm on this 33 MP sensor before trailing shows; tighten to 8–10 s if you pixel-peep the corners. Longer collects more light per frame but softens the point stars.
items.example: At 24 mm you set 12 s for a Milky Way frame at the Marin Headlands, dropping to 10 s when you plan to crop in tight on the core.
items.alt: 20 s — acceptable if you'll downsize for web or stack many frames, trading slight elongation for more signal per exposure.

items.name: Focus
items.value: Manual Focus → Focus Magnifier on a bright star → Peaking
items.why: Focus determines whether the stars are sharp points or soft blobs, and autofocus can't lock on a dim star. Manual Focus with Focus Magnifier lets you punch in on a bright star and rack focus until it shrinks to the smallest point, with Peaking as a secondary confirmation. This is the only reliable way to nail true infinity in the dark.
items.example: You magnify on Vega over Mount Tam, nudge the focus ring until it collapses to a pinpoint, then tape the ring so it won't drift between frames.

items.name: Subject Recog in AF
items.why: Subject Recognition in AF hunts for faces, animals, and vehicles. There is nothing for it to recognize in a star field, and since you're focusing manually anyway it serves no purpose; leaving it off avoids any spurious AF behavior. Pure MF control is what astro demands.
items.example: Composing a dark Point Reyes sky, Off ensures the camera isn't wasting cycles searching for a subject that isn't there.

items.name: White Balance
items.why: White Balance sets the color temperature of the rendered image. A fixed Kelvin around 3800–4200 K via C.Temp./Filter neutralizes the night sky and tames orange light pollution, giving a consistent look across every frame in a stack. Because you shoot RAW, this is only a starting point you can refine non-destructively in post.
items.example: At 4000 K your Milky Way frames over the Marin Headlands render a neutral sky instead of the sodium-orange cast from distant San Francisco glow.
items.alt: 3200 K — cooler, to fight heavier light pollution when shooting closer to the Bay Area's urban skyglow.

items.name: SteadyShot
items.why: SteadyShot is in-body stabilization meant for handheld shake. On a tripod there is no shake to correct, and a live IBIS unit can drift during a multi-second exposure and smear otherwise sharp stars. Off locks the sensor so your point stars stay points.
items.example: For a 20 s Milky Way exposure at Point Reyes, SteadyShot Off keeps the stars from trailing into tiny commas from sensor micro-drift.

items.name: Long Exposure NR
items.value: Off (sequences) / On for a single long frame
items.why: Long Exposure NR captures a dark frame after each shot and subtracts it to remove hot pixels and thermal noise, but it doubles every exposure and requires Mechanical Shutter. For a stacked sequence you turn it Off so there are no dead gaps between frames (stacking software handles the noise instead); it's also auto-disabled in continuous drive. Save it On only for a one-off ultra-long single frame.
items.example: For a 15-frame Milky Way stack you keep it Off so frames fire back-to-back; for a single 4-minute star-trail frame you turn it On to subtract the hot pixels.
items.alt: On — only when you shoot one very long single exposure (e.g. a multi-minute trail) and can afford to double the capture time.

items.name: High ISO NR
items.why: High ISO NR applies in-camera luminance noise reduction to JPEGs and the embedded preview. Set Low or Off so faint stars and the delicate core detail aren't smeared away by aggressive smoothing — you'll denoise far more intelligently in Lightroom or Sequator after stacking. Note RAW data is largely unaffected, but keeping it minimal keeps your previews honest.
items.example: At Off, your ISO 1000 Milky Way frames keep every faint star that an aggressive in-camera denoise would erase before you ever stack them.
items.alt: Normal — acceptable only if you're shooting JPEG-only single frames with no stacking workflow.

items.name: RAW File Type
items.why: RAW File Type sets how the RAW is encoded. Lossless Compressed stores the complete sensor data at a smaller file size with zero quality penalty, and crucially it avoids the lossy compression artifacts that would band the smooth night-sky gradients of the Milky Way. This body offers no Uncompressed option, so Lossless Comp is the top-quality choice.
items.example: Your 20 s core frame in Lossless Comp lets you stretch the faint sky gradient in post without the stair-stepping that lossy RAW would introduce.

items.name: Save to MR3
items.why: This writes the full astro state — Manual mode, ISO 1000, f/2.8, 12 s, Mechanical shutter, MF, fixed Kelvin and NR settings — into the in-body MR3 register. Because MR3 lives in the camera body rather than on a card, the recipe is always available regardless of which card is loaded, and recalls instantly with a dial turn in the dark.
items.example: Arriving at a dark Point Reyes pullout, you spin to MR3 and every astro setting snaps into place without fumbling through menus by red headlamp.

stages.label: C5
stages.title: Daytime Timelapse
stages.subtitle: M2 · card register · RAW intervals + NiSi ND

scenario.tagline: RAW intervals you assemble in post for maximum quality and flexibility.
scenario.fieldNote: Two routes: (a) [Interval Shoot Func.] → RAW stills you assemble in post (your pick), or (b) the in-camera Time-lapse movie (S&Q dial → Time-lapse) → a finished clip, fast but 8-bit with no RAW grading. Post for (a): import RAW to Lightroom, grade one frame, sync to all, export a JPEG/TIFF sequence → Premiere image sequence at 24/30 fps → export. For Reels, also export a 1080×1920 version.

gate.headline: Configure, then save to M2
gate.detail: Manual exposure + [Interval Shoot Func.] → On. Save to card register M2.

items.name: Mode
items.value: Manual; Interval Shoot Func. → Interval Shooting On
items.why: Manual exposure with [Interval Shoot Func.] → [Interval Shooting] On is the engine of this guide: the camera fires a series of full RAW stills on a fixed cadence that you assemble into a clip in post, rather than baking an 8-bit movie in-camera. Manual mode locks shutter, aperture and ISO so brightness never jumps frame-to-frame, which is what keeps a daytime sequence flicker-free. This is the RAW route — maximum grading latitude at the cost of a post workflow.
items.example: A 20-minute sunset over the Marin Headlands: you dial M, set exposure once at golden hour, flip Interval Shooting On, and walk away while the body captures the cloud bank rolling in over the Golden Gate.
items.alt: In-camera Time-lapse movie via the S&Q dial — faster to a finished clip, but 8-bit with no RAW grading, so only choose it when you need the file out the door immediately.

items.name: File Format
items.why: RAW (Lossless Comp) records the full sensor data so you can grade white balance and exposure across the entire sequence in Lightroom — grade one frame, sync to all. On this body there is no Uncompressed RAW option, so Lossless Comp is the highest-quality format available and saves card space versus uncompressed bodies. For a graded daytime timelapse this latitude is the whole point of shooting stills instead of an in-camera movie.
items.example: A coastal fog burn-off where you pull the shadows up two stops and warm the WB 400 K in one master frame, then sync that develop setting across all 400 RAWs before exporting the sequence.
items.alt: RAW & JPEG — adds a ready-to-preview JPEG per frame for quick proofing on the card, at the cost of roughly double the write load and storage.

items.name: Shooting Interval
items.why: Shooting Interval is the start-to-start time between frames and sets how fast the world moves in the final clip; the manual defines it as the time from one exposure's start to the next, range 1–60 s here. Shorter intervals suit fast-moving subjects (wind-driven clouds) so motion stays smooth; longer intervals compress slow scenes. Match it to your subject and your target clip length at 24–30 fps.
items.example: Fast cumulus stacking over Mount Tam: a 3 s interval over 18 minutes yields ~360 frames — a buttery 12 s clip at 30 fps.
items.alt: 6–8 s for slow, high stratus or shadow creep across a valley, where a tighter interval would just waste frames on near-identical motion.

items.name: Shutter time
items.why: Setting the per-frame shutter to roughly half the interval ('shutter drag') adds motion blur to each frame, so moving elements streak slightly and the assembled clip looks fluid instead of stuttery. At a 4 s interval that's about a 2 s exposure — far too long for daylight without cutting light, which is why this pairs with an ND. It is the single biggest factor in cinematic-looking daytime timelapse.
items.example: A 2 s exposure on each frame as waves wash the beach at Pacifica blurs the surf into soft motion trails that flow smoothly from frame to frame.
items.alt: A faster shutter (sharp, crisp frames) if you specifically want a crunchy, stop-motion staccato look — valid stylistically but less smooth.

items.name: ND filter
items.value: NiSi Swift 6-stop (bright) or 10-stop (very bright) + CPL outside
items.why: A fixed-strength ND lets you reach those multi-second daytime shutters at f/8–f/11 and ISO 100 instead of blowing out; a screw/magnetic fixed ND also lets a CPL stack outside it to cut glare and deepen skies. The 6-stop covers bright overcast and the 10-stop handles harsh midday sun. Without it, shutter drag in daylight is impossible.
items.example: High noon at a Sonoma vineyard: the 10-stop drops you to a 2 s exposure at f/11 ISO 100, with the CPL killing reflections off the leaves.
items.alt: A variable ND for fast field adjustment as light changes — convenient, but risks cross-polarization X-patterns on wide skies, so fixed NDs are safer for clean timelapse.

items.name: Aperture / ISO
items.why: f/8–f/11 sits in the lens's sharpness sweet spot and delivers deep depth of field so foreground and horizon are both crisp, while ISO 100 keeps every frame clean with maximum dynamic range. Locking both manually is essential — any auto drift would introduce flicker across the sequence. This is your baseline daytime exposure triangle, with the ND doing the light-cutting.
items.example: A layered ridgeline scene where wildflowers in the near foreground and the distant Sierra crest both stay tack-sharp at f/11, ISO 100 across all 500 frames.
items.alt: f/8 in lower light or with a longer lens to claw back a stop and a little sharpness if diffraction softens f/11 on your particular glass.

items.name: Focus
items.why: Manual Focus set once and left alone prevents the camera from refocusing between frames, which would cause distracting focus pops in the finished clip. Autofocus can hunt as clouds, contrast or light shift over a long run, so you remove that variable entirely. Set focus, confirm with magnification, then don't touch the ring.
items.example: Focusing on a lone cypress at Point Reyes, locking MF, then letting the marine layer drift behind it for 30 minutes without a single focus shift.
items.alt: AF-S with a single locked focus point then switching the lens to MF after the half-press confirms — fine if you trust the lock, but manual is foolproof.

items.name: Shutter Type in Interval
items.why: Electronic Shutter means no mechanical curtain actuates, so across thousands of interval frames you incur zero shutter-mechanism wear and the camera is fully silent. The usual electronic-shutter downsides — rolling shutter and a slight per-frame dynamic-range penalty — are irrelevant on a static tripod with multi-second exposures. The manual confirms Interval defaults to Electronic regardless of the global Shutter Type setting.
items.example: A 600-frame sunset run where the silent electronic shutter clicks through the whole sequence without adding a single cycle to the mechanical shutter's rated life.
items.alt: Mechanical Shutter only if you ever needed flash sync or saw banding under specific artificial lighting — neither applies to a daytime landscape, so keep Electronic.

items.name: AE Tracking Sensitivity
items.why: AE Tracking Sensitivity governs how aggressively auto exposure reacts to luminance changes during interval shooting; per the manual, [Low] makes exposure changes smoother frame-to-frame. For a day run with shifting clouds or a gradual light fall-off, Low prevents abrupt brightness steps that read as flicker. It only matters if you let any part of exposure stay automatic — with fully locked manual it's a safety net.
items.example: A late-afternoon scene where a cloud briefly dims the sun: Low keeps the exposure ramp gentle instead of snapping darker for a few frames and back.
items.alt: Mid if your light is changing faster (a fast sunset 'holy grail' transition) and Low can't keep up with the falling light.

items.name: Shoot Interval Priority
items.why: Shoot Interval Priority keeps the firing cadence locked even when, in an auto exposure mode, the metered shutter would run longer than the interval — the manual notes it applies in P and A modes. It guarantees an even, predictable frame rhythm so your assembled clip plays at constant speed. On a fully manual run it has no effect, but leaving it On is harmless insurance.
items.example: Shooting in A priority into a darkening sky where the meter wants a 5 s shutter on a 4 s interval — Interval Priority caps it so the cadence never stutters.
items.alt: Off if you are fully manual and want the exposure you set respected literally, with no cadence override — functionally equivalent here.

items.name: Number of Shots
items.why: Number of Shots sets how many frames are captured, which directly determines clip length: frames ÷ fps = seconds (300 ÷ 30 = 10 s; ÷ 24 = 12.5 s). The range is 1–9999, so 300–600 gives a comfortable 10–25 s clip with editing headroom. Plan this together with interval and target runtime before you start.
items.example: 450 frames at a 4 s interval runs about 30 minutes of real time and yields a 15 s clip at 30 fps — enough to trim heads and tails in the edit.
items.alt: 600+ shots when you want extra runtime for a long transition or room to crop into the sequence, mindful of battery and card capacity.

items.name: SteadyShot
items.why: SteadyShot Off because the camera is locked on a tripod, where in-body stabilization can actually introduce micro-shifts between frames as it tries to correct a non-existent shake. Disabling it guarantees the framing is pixel-stable across the whole sequence, which is critical when frames are stacked into motion. The factory default is On, so this is a deliberate change for tripod work.
items.example: A locked-off composition of the Bay Bridge at dusk where Off keeps every frame perfectly registered, so the only motion in the clip is the traffic and sky.
items.alt: None for tripod timelapse — leave it Off; you'd only want it On for handheld shooting, which doesn't apply here.

items.name: Power
items.value: USB-PD bank via USB-C ([USB Power Supply] → Auto)
items.why: Powering from a USB-PD bank through USB-C ([USB Power Supply] → Auto) lets a long interval run outlast the internal battery, so a multi-hour sequence never dies mid-capture. The manual confirms an icon appears next to the battery display when USB power is supplied. For unattended daytime runs this removes the single most common cause of a ruined timelapse.
items.example: A 90-minute afternoon-to-golden-hour run powered off a 20,000 mAh PD bank, so the camera shoots all 600 frames without you swapping a battery.
items.alt: A fully charged internal battery alone for shorter runs (under ~30–45 minutes), where the cable-free simplicity outweighs the runtime limit.

items.name: Save to M2 (both cards)
items.why: Camera Set. Memory → M2 writes this entire daytime-timelapse configuration into the M2 register on the mode dial, so the whole rig — Interval settings, ND-friendly exposure, MF, Electronic shutter — recalls with one twist instead of menu-diving in the field. Saving to both cards mirrors the config so either card slot can restore it. M2 is reserved here for daytime; M3 holds the nightlapse.
items.example: Arriving at a viewpoint as clouds build, you rotate to M2 and the camera is instantly configured for the daytime RAW interval run — no menu hunting.

stages.label: C6
stages.title: Nightlapse
stages.subtitle: M3 · card register · low-light / star timelapse

scenario.tagline: Long-exposure frames through the night — lock exposure and let the intervalometer drive.
scenario.fieldNote: Holy-grail sunset→stars: set [AE Tracking Sensitivity] → Low so exposure climbs gradually as it darkens; finish the ramp in Lightroom (LRTimelapse-style deflicker). Cold Bay Area nights drain batteries and fog the front element — run external USB-PD and consider a dew heater. [Auto Pixel Mapping] On helps with thermal hot pixels.

gate.headline: Configure, then save to M3
gate.detail: Manual exposure, MF to infinity in twilight if possible. Then save to card register M3.

items.name: Mode
items.value: Manual; Interval Shoot Func. → Interval Shooting On
items.why: Manual mode with [Interval Shoot Func.] → [Interval Shooting] On locks a fixed long-exposure recipe and lets the intervalometer fire frames through the night, which you assemble into a nightlapse in post. Locking exposure manually is what keeps star brightness and noise consistent frame-to-frame; the camera should never re-meter in the dark. This is the still-frame RAW route for full denoise and grading control.
items.example: A Milky Way core rising over a dark-sky site near Lassen: you set 20 s, f/2.8, ISO 6400 once, flip Interval Shooting On, and let it run for two hours.
items.alt: Holy-grail mode with AE Tracking on Low for a sunset-into-stars run where exposure must ramp as it darkens — manual-only is for pure night once it's fully dark.

items.name: File Format
items.why: RAW (Lossless Comp) preserves every bit of shadow data for the heavy lifting night frames demand — aggressive denoise, big shadow lifts, and star recovery. This body has no Uncompressed RAW, so Lossless Comp is the maximum-quality option and keeps file sizes manageable across hundreds of long exposures. JPEG would fall apart under the grading a nightlapse needs.
items.example: Pulling the foreground ridgeline up three stops out of near-black and running AI denoise on each frame of a Milky Way sequence — only possible with full RAW data.
items.alt: RAW & JPEG to get instant previews for checking focus and framing on the card between test frames, accepting the extra write load.

items.name: Shutter time
items.why: The per-frame shutter gathers starlight; 8–15 s keeps stars as sharp points within NPF limits for your focal length, while going longer deliberately renders star trails. Crucially it must stay shorter than the interval so the camera finishes writing before the next frame is due. Pick it from your lens's focal length and whether you want points or trails.
items.example: On a 20mm lens, a 13 s exposure holds the stars as crisp points while the Milky Way drifts frame to frame; switch to 30 s if you want trailing arcs instead.
items.alt: 20–25 s on a wider 14–16mm lens, where the NPF rule allows a longer exposure before stars begin to streak — gathering more light per frame.

items.name: Interval
items.value: shutter + ~1–2 s buffer (e.g. 13 s shutter → 15 s interval)
items.why: Interval here is set to the shutter time plus a 1–2 s buffer (e.g. 13 s shutter → 15 s interval) so each long RAW finishes writing to the card before the next frame begins, keeping cadence even. If the interval ever fell at or below the shutter time the sequence would skip or stall — fatal for smooth playback. Always make the interval comfortably longer than the exposure.
items.example: A 20 s exposure on a 22 s interval over Death Valley dark skies: the 2 s gap lets each big RAW flush to the CFexpress card before the next star frame fires.
items.alt: A larger buffer (e.g. 20 s shutter → 25 s interval) if you shoot to a slower SD card or RAW+JPEG, where write times run longer.

items.name: Aperture / ISO
items.why: A fast f/2.8 aperture with ISO 3200–6400 collects enough light in seconds to render the Milky Way without resorting to minutes-long exposures that would trail the stars. f/2.8 is the practical bright limit for most astro zooms while staying reasonably sharp into the corners. Lock both manually so brightness and noise stay identical across every frame.
items.example: f/2.8, ISO 6400, 20 s capturing the galactic core's dust lanes over a Sierra alpine lake, bright enough to grade without crushing the foreground to black.
items.alt: ISO 3200 if you have a faster f/1.8–f/2 lens or brighter twilight, trading a bit of brightness for cleaner, lower-noise frames.

items.name: Focus
items.value: Manual Focus to infinity via Focus Magnifier
items.why: Manual Focus to infinity, set precisely with Focus Magnifier on a bright star or distant light, guarantees pinpoint stars — autofocus simply can't lock in near-darkness. Setting it while there's still some twilight makes nailing it far easier, then you leave the ring untouched for the whole run. A hair off infinity turns every star into a soft blob across hundreds of frames.
items.example: During blue hour you punch in 14.4x on Vega with Focus Magnifier, tweak the ring until it's a tight point, tape the ring, and shoot until 2 a.m.
items.alt: Focus on a distant city light or the horizon line at high magnification if no bright star is visible yet — same goal of a confirmed infinity lock.

items.name: Long Exposure NR
items.why: Long Exposure NR must be Off: when On, the camera shoots a matching dark frame after each exposure, effectively doubling every frame's duration and leaving a gap in the cadence — the manual confirms it engages on exposures of 1 s or longer. That stall would punch holes in the nightlapse sequence. Handle the resulting hot pixels in post instead, where [Auto Pixel Mapping] and Lightroom clean them up. Note the factory default is On, so you must turn it off.
items.example: With a 20 s exposure, leaving LENR On would add a 20 s dark frame after every shot, blowing your 22 s interval and dropping frames — Off keeps the cadence intact.
items.alt: None for a nightlapse — it has to be Off; a single long-exposure still is the only time you'd ever leave it On.

items.name: High ISO NR
items.why: High ISO NR set to Low or Off preserves faint stars and fine detail that aggressive in-camera noise reduction would smear into mush, leaving the real denoise to Lightroom where you can balance it per frame. At ISO 3200–6400 the camera's NR is heavy-handed for astro. The factory default is Normal, so this is a deliberate reduction.
items.example: Off keeps the dim outer stars of the Milky Way visible instead of NR softening them away, then Lightroom's AI denoise cleans the sky while protecting those points.
items.alt: Low as a middle ground if you want slightly cleaner straight-out-of-camera previews while still leaving room to denoise in post.

items.name: AE Tracking Sensitivity
items.value: Low (or fully manual, no ISO Auto)
items.why: AE Tracking Sensitivity Low smooths any auto-exposure changes for a gradual sunset-to-stars ramp; for pure night you instead go fully manual with no ISO Auto so brightness is frozen across the run. Low prevents abrupt brightness steps during a transition, while locked manual eliminates them entirely once it's dark. Choose based on whether your run spans a light change or stays in full night.
items.example: For a holy-grail run starting at dusk, Low lets exposure climb gently as the sky darkens; for a 1 a.m. core sequence you lock manual so every frame is identical.
items.alt: Fully manual with ISO fixed (no ISO Auto) for any run that's entirely in the dark — the cleanest, most predictable result.

items.name: Shutter Type in Interval
items.why: Electronic Shutter avoids all mechanical shutter actuation across a long overnight run, so there's zero curtain wear over hundreds of frames and the camera stays silent. Rolling shutter is irrelevant for static multi-second star exposures on a tripod. The manual confirms Interval shooting defaults to Electronic regardless of the global Shutter Type.
items.note: See the astro note — the manual doesn’t address “star-eater” long-exposure NR; verify at your exposure lengths.
items.example: A 400-frame, three-hour Milky Way run firing silently on the electronic shutter, sparing the mechanical shutter hundreds of cycles in a single night.
items.alt: Mechanical only if a specific lens or scene showed electronic-shutter banding from a distant light source — rare in astro, so keep Electronic.

items.name: White Balance
items.why: A fixed C.Temp./Filter white balance around 3800–4200 K holds night color constant across every frame so there's no color flicker, and it renders a natural dark-blue sky rather than the orange cast of Auto WB. Locking it manually is essential because Auto WB would wander frame to frame. Fine-tune the exact Kelvin to your light pollution in post.
items.example: 4000 K keeps the sky a clean deep blue and the Milky Way neutral over a Bay Area night, instead of Auto WB drifting warm whenever the core brightens.
items.alt: 3800 K under heavier city skyglow to counter the orange sodium-light cast pushing into the lower sky.

items.name: Power
items.why: External USB-PD power is mandatory for long night runs because cold Bay Area nights drain the internal battery fast, and a dew heater stops the front element from fogging over partway through the sequence. A dead battery or a dewed lens ruins hours of capture. Pair the PD bank with the dew strap for unattended overnight reliability; pair this with Auto Power OFF Temp set to High so the camera doesn't shut down on warm gear.
items.example: A coastal Pacifica night at 8°C: a USB-PD bank powers a three-hour run while a dew heater band keeps condensation off the 20mm front element the whole time.
items.alt: A grip with dual batteries plus a dew heater for shorter sessions where you'd rather avoid a dangling USB cable, accepting the cold-drain risk.

items.name: Save to M3 (both cards)
items.why: Camera Set. Memory → M3 writes the full nightlapse rig — long-exposure manual values, LENR Off, fixed WB, Electronic shutter, low NR — into the M3 register so you can recall the entire astro setup with one dial twist in the dark. Saving to both cards mirrors it for either slot. M3 is reserved here for night, keeping it cleanly separate from the daytime M2 preset.
items.example: As the Milky Way clears the ridge, you rotate to M3 and the camera is instantly armed for the long-exposure interval run — no fumbling through menus by headlamp.

stages.label: C7
stages.title: Events
stages.subtitle: M4 · card register · run-and-gun video + stills

scenario.tagline: Speed for stills, a locked look for video — silent shooting when the room is quiet.
scenario.fieldNote: Under LED/fluorescent, the electronic shutter can band — turn [Anti-flicker Shoot.] On (mechanical) or use [Var. Shutter] (electronic) in [Anti-flicker Set.], or switch to the mechanical shutter. Watch ISO; S-Cinetone behaves around a low base ISO.

gate.headline: Configure, then save to M4
gate.detail: A hybrid: Aperture Priority for stills, Manual + ISO Auto for video. Save to card register M4.

dials.label: Mode dial → A (stills) — video uses M + ISO Auto

items.name: Exposure mode
items.why: Exposure mode sets how aperture, shutter, and ISO are decided. For events you run a split brain: Aperture Priority on stills lets you dial depth of field (open wide for ceremony portraits) while the camera chases shutter and ISO, and Manual + ISO Auto on video locks your shutter angle and aperture for a consistent look while ISO floats with the changing room light. This keeps stills fast and reactive while video stays visually stable shot to shot.
items.example: During a candle-lit first dance you hold f/2 in Aperture Priority for stills, then flip to video in Manual at 1/50 + ISO Auto so the moving spotlights don't make your exposure pump.
items.alt: Shutter Priority for stills if you care more about freezing motion than depth of field — useful for fast processionals where 1/250 is non-negotiable.

items.name: Drive (stills)
items.why: Drive mode sets the burst behavior of the shutter. Continuous Shooting Hi gives you a fast-but-manageable burst that catches fleeting expressions and gesture peaks without flooding your card the way the full electronic 30 fps would. It keeps your buffer healthy and your cull short — critical when you're delivering a wedding gallery on a deadline.
items.example: As the couple turns from the altar laughing, a Hi burst grabs three or four frames of the real smile instead of one stiff one.
items.alt: Cont. Shooting Mid if you want even tighter selects and a slower buffer drain during long receptions.

items.name: Shutter Type
items.why: Shutter Type chooses between the silent electronic shutter and the mechanical shutter. Electronic shutter lets you shoot with zero shutter noise during reverent moments, while mechanical is your fallback when fast motion or banding light would otherwise distort the frame. Note that turning on Silent Mode force-locks the camera to electronic shutter and also mutes the AF beeps and other sounds.
items.example: During the silent exchange of vows you switch to electronic shutter so the room hears nothing while you fire a burst inches from the couple.
items.alt: Mechanical Shutter for the loud, fast-moving reception, where its global readout resists rolling-shutter skew on quick pans and avoids LED banding.

items.name: Anti-flicker
items.value: Anti-flicker Shoot. On (mech) or Var. Shutter (elec)
items.why: Anti-flicker counters the strobing of LED and fluorescent lighting that otherwise paints horizontal banding across your frames. Anti-flicker Shoot. On times the exposure to the light's flicker but only works with the mechanical shutter, whereas Var. Shutter fine-tunes the shutter speed to cancel banding and works with the electronic shutter. Both live under Shooting > Shutter/Silent > Anti-flicker Set.
items.example: At an LED-uplit ballroom your silent electronic-shutter frames show rolling dark bands, so you enable Var. Shutter and dial the shutter until the bands vanish while staying silent.
items.alt: Anti-flicker Shoot. On if you've already accepted mechanical shutter for that part of the night — it's simpler and fully automatic.

items.name: Focus Mode / Area
items.why: Focus Mode and Area decide how the camera acquires and holds focus. Continuous AF keeps tracking moving subjects in real time, and a Wide (or Tracking: Wide) area lets the camera's subject recognition hunt for faces anywhere in the frame instead of a small box you have to aim. For unpredictable event movement this means you compose freely and let recognition lock the right person.
items.example: As the flower girl runs down the aisle unpredictably, Wide + AF-C lets recognition grab her eye wherever she darts in the frame.
items.alt: Tracking: Spot if you need to deliberately pick one person in a dense crowd and have the camera stick to them as they move.

items.name: Recognition Target
items.why: Recognition Target tells the subject-recognition AF what to look for. Set to Human, it detects faces and eyes and keeps focus pinned to a person even as they turn their head away or are partially blocked. At an event full of people this is what keeps your couple, not the background guest, tack sharp.
items.example: During the toast the best man turns to gesture at the crowd; Human recognition holds his eye through the profile turn instead of jumping to the table behind him.
items.alt: Auto if you're mixing in pets or details — it lets the camera pick human or animal automatically without you switching modes.

items.name: ISO AUTO Min. SS
items.why: ISO AUTO Min. SS sets the slowest shutter the camera will use before it starts raising ISO in an Auto-ISO mode. Pinning it around 1/160–1/250 guarantees candid motion stays frozen in dim, mixed venue light rather than smearing as the camera tries to protect ISO. It trades a bit more noise for consistently sharp moments you can't reshoot.
items.example: In a dim reception hall the camera wants 1/60 and soft hands; with Min SS at 1/250 it instead bumps ISO and freezes the couple mid-spin sharply.
items.alt: Faster (1/500) for very active receptions or kids running, accepting higher ISO for guaranteed sharpness.

items.name: Picture Profile (video)
items.why: Picture Profile sets the video gamma and color science. PP11 (S-Cinetone) delivers Sony's broadcast-tuned, flattering skin-tone look essentially graded in-camera, so event video is delivery-ready with little to no color work. For a fast turnaround it saves you the S-Log grading round-trip while still looking cinematic.
items.example: You hand off ceremony clips the next morning straight from S-Cinetone, with warm, even skin tones, no LUT pass needed.
items.alt: S-Log3 (PP-based) if a particular event needs maximum dynamic range and you have time to grade — better for high-contrast window-lit venues.

items.name: Video format
items.why: Video format sets resolution, frame rate, and color depth. XAVC S 4K at 24p/30p in 10-bit 4:2:2 gives you a sharp, gradeable, broadcast-quality file that's still light enough to edit smoothly and matches typical 4K delivery. The 10-bit 4:2:2 headroom protects skin tones and gradients when you tweak the S-Cinetone footage.
items.example: You cut a 4K highlight reel of the ceremony in 24p for that filmic cadence, with enough color depth to nudge the white balance of a tungsten-lit aisle without banding.
items.alt: XAVC S-I (intra-frame) if you want frame-accurate editing and maximum quality and have a fast card and storage to spare.

items.name: White Balance
items.why: White Balance sets how the camera neutralizes color casts. Auto WB adapts shot to shot through the constantly shifting mixed lighting of a venue, keeping a usable baseline without you riding a dial, while a custom WB locks color when a room is strongly tinted and AWB drifts. For events, AWB's consistency usually wins, with custom held in reserve.
items.example: Walking from a daylight garden ceremony into a tungsten reception, AWB keeps skin tones believable across both without you stopping to reset.
items.alt: Custom (gray-card) WB when a venue bathes everyone in heavy magenta or green LED wash that AWB can't fully tame.

items.name: SteadyShot
items.why: SteadyShot is the in-body stabilization. Active mode adds electronic correction on top of the sensor-shift IS to smooth handheld walking video, while On (standard) stabilizes stills without the slight crop Active introduces. This lets you move with the action and still hand off footage that isn't jittery.
items.example: You follow the couple's exit down a staircase handheld; Active mode irons out your footfalls so the clip looks gimbal-like.
items.alt: Dynamic Active for even smoother walking video if you can accept a larger crop — useful for wide-lens follow shots.

items.name: Save to M4 (both cards)
items.why: This writes the entire events hybrid configuration into the camera's M4 register so you can recall the whole stills-plus-video setup with one dial move. Saving to both cards means the preset survives even if you swap or reformat one card. It turns a long menu setup into an instant, repeatable recall at the next event.
items.example: Arriving at the next wedding you rotate to M4 and the silent-shutter, AF-C Human, S-Cinetone rig is live in one click — no re-diving the menus in the lobby.

scenario.tagline: The camera conforms slow-mo in-camera to your timeline — no MR slot spent.
scenario.fieldNote: Audio isn’t recorded in S&Q. 240p FHD is softer than 4K — light it well. 120p in 4K means the S35 crop applies, so the 200-600 reaches further but the 24 mm becomes ~36 mm-equivalent. Rolling shutter is higher at the highest rates — avoid fast whip pans.

gate.headline: Move the lower dial to S&Q
gate.detail: Slow-Motion lives on the dedicated S&Q dial position — its record settings are remembered within S&Q mode. You don’t spend an MR slot on it.
gate.note: No Camera Set. Memory save needed — settings persist in S&Q.

items.name: Mode
items.why: Mode here means putting the camera into Slow & Quick Motion via the Still/Movie/S&Q dial. S&Q conforms the high-speed capture to your project frame rate in-camera, so the file plays back already slowed — no re-timing in post. For nature b-roll this means a 120 fps capture lands on your timeline as finished 5x slow-motion.
items.example: You frame a hummingbird at a feeder, hit record in S&Q, and the clip drops into your edit already smooth slow-motion, ready to cut.

items.name: 4K 120p
items.why: This sets 120 fps capture conformed to a 24p project for 5x slow motion at 4K resolution. It's the sharpest slow-mo the camera offers, but 4K maxes out at 120p and the high readout forces an APS-C/S35 crop, so you must frame wider than the final shot. The high bitrate also wants a fast card.
items.example: A bee lifting off a blooming poppy at 5x slow-mo in crisp 4K — you frame loose because the S35 crop turns your 24mm into roughly a 36mm view.
items.alt: FHD 120p if you want the full-frame field of view and a lighter file, trading 4K sharpness for no crop.

items.name: FHD 240p
items.why: This selects 240 fps capture conformed to 24p for dramatic 10x slow motion. 240 fps is FHD-only — it's blocked for every 4K format (XAVC HS 4K, XAVC S 4K, and XAVC S-I 4K) — and it still applies the S35 crop, so footage is softer and needs more light. Use it for the most extreme slowdowns where motion detail matters more than resolution.
items.example: A water droplet splashing off a leaf stretched to 10x slow-mo, where 240 fps reveals the crown of the splash that 120 would blur past.
items.alt: FHD 120p (5x) when you want cleaner, less noisy footage and 240's extra slowdown isn't necessary.

items.name: Shutter
items.why: Shutter controls motion blur per frame, and the 180-degree rule is set against the capture rate, not the project rate. At 120 fps that means roughly 1/250 s and at 240 fps roughly 1/500 s, giving each slowed frame a natural amount of blur instead of a stuttery, over-sharp look. Locking it manually keeps the slow-mo cadence smooth.
items.example: Filming windblown grass at 120 fps, 1/250 keeps each blade softly trailing rather than strobing harshly when slowed to 5x.
items.alt: A faster shutter (e.g. 1/1000) deliberately, when you want crisp, staccato detail like individual water droplets frozen in each frame.

items.name: Picture Profile
items.why: Picture Profile sets the color and gamma so your slow-mo b-roll matches the rest of the project. PP11 (S-Cinetone) gives a graded-looking image straight out of camera, while S-Log3 maximizes dynamic range for high-contrast nature scenes you'll grade later. Pick whichever your main footage uses so the slow-mo cuts in seamlessly.
items.example: Your event film uses S-Cinetone, so you shoot the 5x slow-mo of cherry blossoms in PP11 too, and the inserts match without a grade.
items.alt: S-Log3 when the scene has bright sky and deep shade and you want grading latitude — just commit to the LUT/grade in post.

items.name: SteadyShot
items.why: SteadyShot stabilizes handheld slow-mo footage. Active is available even at 4K 120p, keeping handheld nature shots usable (on top of the existing S35 crop), though the more aggressive Dynamic Active stabilization is unavailable at the highest frame rates. Slow motion also magnifies handshake, so any stabilization here pays off doubly.
items.example: Handholding a 120p shot of a swaying flower, Active keeps the frame steady enough that the 5x slowdown doesn't amplify your micro-jitters into seasickness.
items.alt: Off on a tripod or gimbal, to avoid any extra crop and let the rig do the stabilizing.

items.name: Focus Mode
items.why: Focus Mode keeps the subject sharp through the action, with Continuous AF tracking movement and subject recognition added when the subject suits it. In slow motion any focus miss is stretched out and obvious on screen, so reliable tracking matters more than usual. Recognition (e.g. animal/bird) helps lock onto wildlife that drifts within the frame.
items.example: A butterfly creeping across a flower at 5x slow-mo stays sharp as AF-C with animal recognition rides its eye through the slowed crawl.
items.alt: Manual focus for a locked-off macro shot like a static blooming bud, where you prefer a fixed focus plane over AF hunting.

items.name: No save needed
items.why: There's nothing to save to an MR/M register here: S&Q is its own dedicated position on the Still/Movie/S&Q dial, and its record settings (frame rate, format, profile) persist with that mode. You just confirm the setup and shoot — no memory slot is consumed, leaving M1–M4 free for your stills/video hybrids. Note that audio is never recorded in S&Q regardless.
items.example: After dialing in 4K 120p you simply flip back to S&Q anytime and your slow-mo settings are still there, with the events M4 preset untouched.

stages.title: Delivery
stages.subtitle: Export recipes — YouTube & Instagram
stages.blurb: Match your capture to your pipeline so the master is what you see — no surprises in the grade.

blocks.heading: YouTube (horizontal 4K)
body: Frame rate: 24p for cinematic narrative; 30p for talking-head/clarity; 60p when you want slow-mo headroom or smooth motion. Deliver at the capture rate; don’t conform 60p to 24p unless you want slow motion.

Codec/quality: XAVC S 4K 10-bit 4:2:2 (Long-GOP) is the practical choice — light on the M4 Max, excellent quality. Use XAVC S-I 4K (All-Intra) for heavy grading or lots of speed-ramping. Per the manual it’s ~240 Mbps at 24p, ~300 at 30p, ~600 at 60p.
S-Log3 vs S-Cinetone: S-Cinetone (PP11) for fast-turnaround content — finished SOOC, grades minimally. S-Log3 (Log Shooting → On (Flexible ISO)) when you want maximum grading latitude.

Exposing S-Log3: Sony documents Flexible ISO; the manual doesn’t publish a base-ISO figure (the “800/8000” you’ll read is from reviews). Use [Display LUT] On (s709 via Select LUT) so the monitor shows a normal image, and [Zebra Display] (~94–95 on a known white) to anchor exposure. Grade in Premiere with a CST/LUT to Rec.709.
Color management: standardise your output to Rec.709 Gamma 2.4 — set the Premiere sequence/monitor accordingly so the YouTube master matches what you see.

blocks.heading: Instagram (feed + vertical Reels)
body: Feed: 1080-wide, 4:5 or 1:1 crops from your 4K horizontal masters. Shoot a little loose to allow reframing.
Reels (vertical): a Premiere export of H.264 High Profile, 1080×1920, 30 fps, VBR 2-pass ~20–24 Mbps, Max Render Quality on, Rec.709 Gamma 2.4 matches IG’s specs well.

Capture at 4K so you can reframe 16:9 → 9:16 with room to pan/scale (a 4K frame downsampled into 1080×1920 stays sharp). The A7 V has no open-gate/4:3 mode — vertical comes from cropping a horizontal 4K frame (or physically shooting vertical).
Shoot 30p or 60p for Reels (60p lets you stabilise/slow slightly and still deliver 30p). Avoid 24p — conforming 24→30 adds judder.
For timelapse Reels, render the assembled sequence to a 4K master first, then a second 1080×1920 export.
S-Cinetone is the pragmatic choice for social — consistent colour with near-zero grading keeps your posting cadence fast; reserve S-Log3 for hero pieces.
